{"design":"# Charge / Set-vs-Charge + Round Tracking Design

## Overview

Full implementation of OSRIC 3.0 §1.6.2.1 (Charging Into Combat) and §1.6.2.10 (Set Weapon Against Charge), plus the general-purpose round/time tracking infrastructure needed to support it (and spell durations, memorization, etc.).

## Game Time Model

### Unified counter (segments as base unit)
A single monotonically increasing int representing game time in segments:
- 1 round = 10 segments
- 1 turn = 100 segments (10 rounds)
- 1 hour = 600 segments
- 1 day = 14,400 segments

Comparison and arithmetic are trivial int ops. Structured accessors (to_round, to_turn, etc.) provide readability.

### Surprise is off-clock
Surprise segments happen before the game clock starts. The surprise resolution code manages segment-scoped effects locally (knows exactly how many surprise segments there are). The game clock begins at round 1, segment 1 (counter value 0 or 1).

Effects that need to carry from surprise into combat get promoted to round-based durations by the surprise code.

### Duration enum (expanded)
```
enum Duration {
    end_of_round,
    segments(count: int),   // managed locally (surprise, casting), not via game clock
    rounds(count: int),
    turns(count: int),
    hours(count: int),
    days(count: int),
    indefinite
}
```

`segments(n)` exists as a variant but is handled by local code (surprise resolution, casting time tracking) rather than the game clock expiry system. All other timed variants map to segment counts for clock-based expiry.

### Duration to segments mapping
```
derive duration_to_segments(d: Duration) -> option<int> {
    match d {
        Duration.end_of_round => some(10),
        Duration.segments(n)  => some(n),       // only used by local code
        Duration.rounds(n)    => some(n * 10),
        Duration.turns(n)     => some(n * 100),
        Duration.hours(n)     => some(n * 600),
        Duration.days(n)      => some(n * 14400),
        Duration.indefinite   => none
    }
}
```

## Condition Expiry (DSL-side)

### Interpreter changes
- Add `applied_at: i64` field to `ActiveCondition` (Rust struct)
- Add `env.current_game_time: Option<i64>` to interpreter Env
- `apply_condition` stamps `applied_at` from `env.current_game_time`
- `to_value()` exposes `applied_at` as an int field to DSL code
- Host sets `env.current_game_time` before calling DSL functions

### DSL-side expiry function
```
function expire_conditions(game_time: int, combatants: list<Character>) {
    for c in combatants {
        for cond in conditions(c) {
            let limit = duration_to_segments(cond.duration)
            if limit.is_some() {
                let elapsed = game_time - cond.applied_at
                if elapsed >= limit.unwrap() {
                    remove_condition(c, cond)
                }
            }
        }
    }
}
```

### Round resolution integration
```
function resolve_round(
    game_time: int,          // NEW: current game time in segments
    round_number: int,       // NEW: for fighter attack alternation, cooldowns
    initiative: InitiativeResult,
    actions: list<RoundAction>
) {
    emit RoundStart(round: round_number, game_time: game_time)
    // ... existing segment loop (for seg in 1..=10) ...
    emit RoundEnd(round: round_number, game_time: game_time)
    // Expire timed conditions at round end
    // (combatant list derived from actions)
}
```

## Charge Rules (OSRIC 3.0 §1.6.2.1)

### What's needed
1. Weapon length data: add `length` field to MeleeWeaponDef (from Table 1.4.2.3c)
2. Charging condition: strips DEX AC bonus, -1 AC penalty if no DEX bonus
3. Charge cooldown: once per 10 rounds, tracked via `last_charge_time` entity field
4. Longer weapon first strike: if defender has longer weapon, attacks before charger
5. Encumbrance restriction: max encumbrance cannot charge unless mounted
6. Mounted lance double: lance + warhorse doubles weapon damage (not STR/magic/spec)

### Charge-vs-Set interaction (resolved in ChargeAction branch of resolve_action)
When ChargeAction resolves:
1. Apply Charging condition (loses DEX AC)
2. Check if target declared SetAgainstCharge in same round
3. If set: defender strikes first with 2x damage
4. If no set but defender has longer weapon: defender strikes first (normal damage)
5. If charger survives: resolve charge attack with +2 to-hit
6. Record last_charge_time for cooldown tracking

### Set-vs-Charge (§1.6.2.10)
- Set weapon readied at segment 1 (already implemented)
- Cannot attack unless actually charged
- Valid weapons: spear, javelin, lance (dismounted), pole arm, trident (already implemented)
- Double damage on hit against charger"}
old_value: {"id":"tdsl-ianf","title":"Charge and set-vs-charge rules are only partially implemented","description":"osric/osric_combat.ttrpg Charge action only applies +2 to hit; osric/osric_initiative.ttrpg has set-vs-charge as a multiplier derive but not full timing/interaction. OSRIC 3.0 sections 1.6.2.1 and 1.6.2.10 require: defender with longer weapon attacks first, charger loses DEX AC bonus, charge frequency limit (once per 10 rounds), and set weapon strike in segment 1 for double damage. Implement full charge/set interaction semantics.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","owner":"tfgast@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-03-03T03:51:02Z","created_by":"Theodore Gast","updated_at":"2026-03-03T03:51:02Z"}

{"design":"## Data Model

### New types (osric_core.ttrpg)

```
struct ClassLevel {
    class: Class
    level: int
    xp: int
}

enum ClassingMode { Single, Multi, Dual }
```

### Character entity changes

Remove:
- `class: Class`
- `level: int = 1`
- `xp: int = 0`

Add:
- `classes: list<ClassLevel>`
- `classing_mode: ClassingMode`

### Accessor conventions

- `first(char.classes).unwrap()` — primary/only class (Single), or first class (Multi/Dual original)
- `last(char.classes).unwrap()` — current class for Dual, same as first for Single
- `max([...])` / iteration — best-of aggregation for BTHB, saves

### Rule divergence by mode

| Mechanic | Single | Multi | Dual |
|---|---|---|---|
| HP | class HD | averaged across classes | summed |
| BTHB | class table | best across classes | best across classes |
| Saves | class table | best per category | best per category |
| Armour | class permission | most restrictive | current class only (while dormant) |
| XP gain | all to class | split evenly | all to current class |
| Abilities | all active | all active | old dormant until new level > old level |

### Dual-class dormancy

Derived, not stored: `classes[1].level <= classes[0].level` means original class is dormant. No extra field needed.

### Files requiring changes

1. **osric_core** — entity + new types
2. **osric_combat** — `effective_target_ac` Monk checks, `resolve_melee/missile_attack` BTHB + Monk damage (use best_bthb, check any class == Monk)
3. **osric_saves** — `saving_throws_for` → aggregate best per category
4. **osric_character** — `prime_req_xp_bonus` → per-class or primary class
5. **osric_class** — `check_level_up` → per ClassLevel entry
6. **osric_initiative** — `has_fighter_multi_attack` → check any fighter-group class

### New aggregation derives needed

- `best_bthb(char) -> int` — max BTHB across all classes
- `best_saves(char) -> SavingThrows` — best per save category
- `effective_armour_permission(char) -> ArmourPermission` — mode-dependent
- `is_class(char, class) -> bool` — true if any ClassLevel matches (replaces `char.class == X`)
- `class_level(char, class) -> option<int>` — level for a specific class if held
- `has_active_class(char, class) -> bool` — like is_class but respects dual-class dormancy
- `primary_class(char) -> ClassLevel` — first entry (original/only)
- `current_class(char) -> ClassLevel` — last entry (active for dual, same as primary for single/multi)"}
old_value: {"id":"tdsl-l7fc","title":"Design: multi-class data model for Character entity","description":"Character currently has class: Class (single) and level: int (single). Multi-classing needs 2-3 simultaneous classes, dual-classing needs old+new class with level tracking. Proposed: change to classes: map\u003cClass, int\u003e mapping class to level. All derives that use class/level (bthb, saving_throws_for, xp_for_level, fighter_attacks_per_round, spell slots, etc.) must iterate over all held classes and pick best or aggregate. Consider: primary_class field for XP allocation, active_class for dual-classing restrictions, HP calculation (roll per class, divide by num classes). This touches osric_core, osric_class, osric_combat, osric_saves, osric_character, osric_initiative.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"feature","owner":"tfgast@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-03-04T06:00:19Z","created_by":"Theodore Gast","updated_at":"2026-03-04T06:00:19Z"}

- `current_class(char) -> ClassLevel` — last entry (active for dual, same as primary for single/multi)","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"feature","owner":"tfgast@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-03-04T06:00:19Z","created_by":"Theodore Gast","updated_at":"2026-03-04T06:25:47Z"}

{"design":"## Equipment State Design

### New Types (osric_equipment.ttrpg)

```
enum WieldedItem {
    Melee(weapon: MeleeWeapon)
    Missile(weapon: MissileWeapon)
    // Future: Wand, Rod, Staff-as-implement, etc.
}

struct Armor {
    armour_type: ArmourType
    // Future: magical_bonus, instance_id, name, etc.
}

struct Shield {
    shield_type: ShieldType
    // Future: magical_bonus, instance_id, etc.
}
```

### Character Entity Changes (osric_core.ttrpg)

Add fields:
    wielded_main: option<WieldedItem> = none
    wielded_off: option<WieldedItem> = none
    worn_armor: option<Armor> = none
    worn_shield: option<Shield> = none

Remove fields (replaced by derives):
    armor_ac: int = 10
    shield_ac_bonus: int = 0
    armour_movement_cap: Feet = 0ft

### New Derives

Equipment extraction helpers (osric_equipment.ttrpg):
    wielded_melee(char) -> option<MeleeWeapon>     — extract Melee variant from wielded_main
    wielded_missile(char) -> option<MissileWeapon>  — extract Missile variant from wielded_main
    wielded_off_melee(char) -> option<MeleeWeapon>  — extract Melee variant from wielded_off

AC/movement derives (osric_combat.ttrpg, near effective_target_ac):
    base_armor_ac(char) -> int          — worn_armor → armour_def().ascending_ac, else 10
    base_shield_bonus(char) -> int      — worn_shield → shield_def().ac_bonus, else 0
    armor_movement_cap(char) -> Feet    — worn_armor → armour_def().movement_cap, else 0ft

effective_target_ac updated to call base_armor_ac / base_shield_bonus instead of reading entity fields.

### Equip/Unequip Functions (osric_equipment.ttrpg)

Hands validation rules:
    - Two-handed main weapon → off-hand must be none, shield must be none
    - Shield → off-hand must be none
    - Off-hand weapon → shield must be none, main must not be two-handed

function equip_main(char, item: WieldedItem)
    If item is two-handed (check via weapon def): clear off-hand, clear shield.
    Set wielded_main. Update current_weight.

function equip_off(char, item: WieldedItem)
    Validate main isn't two-handed. Clear shield.
    Set wielded_off. Update current_weight.

function equip_armor(char, armor: Armor)
    Set worn_armor. Update current_weight + encumbrance.

function equip_shield(char, shield: Shield)
    Validate off-hand is free. Set worn_shield. Update current_weight.

function unequip_main/off/armor/shield(char)
    Clear slot. Update current_weight (+ encumbrance for armor).

### Combat Action Changes (osric_combat.ttrpg)

Actions lose weapon parameter, read from entity state:
    MeleeAttack(target, weapon)  → MeleeAttack(target)    — reads wielded_melee(attacker)
    MissileAttack(target, weapon, distance) → MissileAttack(target, distance) — reads wielded_missile(attacker)
    Charge(target, weapon)       → Charge(target)          — reads wielded_melee(attacker)

requires blocks validate weapon is equipped.
Mechanics (resolve_melee_attack etc.) keep weapon param — pure computation, no entity read.

### Initiative System Changes (osric_initiative.ttrpg)

RoundAction keeps weapon_melee/weapon_missile fields (needed for speed factor calc at declaration time).

resolve_action flow changes:
    1. Equip declared weapon (equip_main) before calling action
    2. Action reads weapon from entity state
    3. No weapon param in action call

### Migration Notes

- All code setting armor_ac / shield_ac_bonus / armour_movement_cap directly must migrate to equip_armor / equip_shield calls
- REPL spawn commands that set these fields need updating
- Test files that set raw AC values need updating
- Encumbrance derive (character_encumbrance) needs update if it reads armour_movement_cap field"}

{"design":"## Design: restricted field modifier

### Summary
Add a `restricted` keyword modifier on entity/group fields that prevents mutation from outside the declaring module (system block). Read access is always allowed. This enforces encapsulation — resource mutations must go through actions exported by the declaring module.

### Syntax
```
entity Character {
    restricted HP: resource(0..=max_HP)
    restricted spell_slots: map<int, resource(0..=9)>
    name: string  // unrestricted, anyone can mutate
}

group HasHP {
    restricted hp: resource(0..=max_hp)
    max_hp: int
}
```

### Semantics
- `restricted` is opt-in per field (default: unrestricted)
- Applies to any entity or group field, not just resources
- **Declaring module** = the `system "Name" { ... }` block containing the field declaration
- Multiple `system "Name"` blocks with same name merge — all share write access
- Read access is always allowed from any module
- Assignment (`=`, `+=`, `-=`) to a restricted field is a **checker error** if the assignment statement is outside the declaring module

### Exceptions
- `grant entity.Group { field_inits }` is allowed cross-module (initialization, not ongoing mutation)
- `revoke entity.Group` is allowed cross-module (removes entire group, not field-level mutation)
- REPL direct assignment (`set`) bypasses restriction (debug tool)

### Module ownership for groups
If system A declares a group with a restricted field, and system B includes that group on an entity, **system A owns the restriction**. System B must call actions from system A to mutate the field. This is intentional: the group author controls the mutation contract.

### Error message
```
error: cannot mutate restricted field `HP` outside declaring module
  --> spells.tdsl:42:9
   |
42 |     target.HP = 0
   |     ^^^^^^^^^^^^ mutation not allowed here
   |
   = note: `HP` is declared as restricted in system "Core Combat"
   = help: use an action from "Core Combat" to modify this field
```

### Audit result
No cross-module mutations exist in the current codebase. This feature is forward-looking protection with zero migration burden."}
old_value: {"id":"tdsl-a5y0","title":"Design: resource threshold triggers","description":"No way to automatically react when a resource crosses a boundary. Currently threshold checks are manual in every action that modifies HP (TakeDamage checks hp\u003c=0). If HP is modified any other way (healing overflow, direct assignment, level drain), checks are missed. Two design options: (A) on-threshold syntax on entity resource fields: 'on hp \u003c= 0 { ... }', or (B) auto-emit ResourceChanged event whenever interpreter mutates a resource, letting hooks react. Option B is lighter-weight and more composable. Blocks: auto-unconscious at 0 HP, auto-death at -10 HP, exhaustion thresholds, spell slot depletion triggers.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"feature","owner":"tfgast@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-03-04T06:00:14Z","created_by":"Theodore Gast","updated_at":"2026-03-04T06:00:14Z"}

{"design":"# Condition Lifecycle Hooks: on_apply / on_remove (Phase 1)

> on_tick is deferred to a second design pass — it requires resolving
> game_time vs turn-relative timing semantics first.

## Motivation

Conditions are purely declarative today (modify + suppress). They cannot
execute imperative code when applied or removed. This blocks use cases like:
- Dropping held items when Unconscious is applied
- Breaking concentration when Stunned is applied
- Emitting status change events for UI/logging
- Cleaning up entity state when a condition wears off

Current workaround — orphan hooks that check `conditions(entity)` on
turn_start — separates behavior from its definition and requires the host
to fire the right events.

## Design Principle: extends over apply_condition

Conditions that imply other conditions should use `extends`, not
imperative `apply_condition()` inside on_apply:

```
// GOOD: declarative relationship
condition Unconscious extends Incapacitated, Prone on bearer: Character { ... }

// BAD: imperative sub-condition management
condition Unconscious on bearer: Character {
    on_apply { apply_condition(bearer, Prone, indefinite) }  // forbidden
}
```

`extends` gives automatic inheritance of modify/suppress clauses and
automatic cleanup on removal. No invocation ownership questions.

## Syntax

New `ConditionClause` variants alongside `Modify` and `Suppress`.
At most one `on_apply` and one `on_remove` per condition declaration.

```
condition Burning(damage: int) on bearer: Character {
    on_apply {
        emit StatusGained(target: bearer, status: "Burning")
        bearer.took_fire_damage = true
    }

    on_remove {
        emit StatusLost(target: bearer, status: "Burning")
    }

    modify fire_resistance(target: bearer) { ... }
    suppress regeneration(target: bearer)
}
```

## Trigger Points

**on_apply** fires immediately after `apply_condition()` succeeds.
The condition is already active — its modify/suppress clauses are in
effect during on_apply execution.

**on_remove** fires immediately before the condition is stripped.
The condition's modify/suppress clauses are still in effect during
on_remove execution. Fires on:
- `remove_condition()` — explicit removal
- `revoke(invocation)` — bulk removal by invocation (on_remove fires
  individually for each condition being removed)
- Future auto-expiry (when on_tick is implemented)

## Capabilities

Lifecycle blocks have **hook-like** execution semantics:
- Can mutate entity state
- Can emit events
- Can roll dice
- Can call derives, mechanics, functions

## Restrictions (enforced)

The following are **forbidden** inside on_apply / on_remove blocks:
- `apply_condition()` — prevents reentrancy and condition graph antipattern
- `remove_condition()` — same reasoning
- `revoke(invocation)` — removes conditions, same restriction

`revoke entity.Group` (optional group revocation) is **allowed** — that
manipulates entity structure, not conditions.

These restrictions are enforced at the checker level (static analysis)
where possible, with a runtime guard as fallback for dynamic dispatch.

Rationale: if a use case arises that genuinely needs condition
manipulation inside lifecycle blocks, we can lift the restriction then
with a concrete motivating example to design around.

## Scoping

- `bearer` (the receiver) is in scope
- Condition parameters are in scope (e.g., `damage` for `Burning(damage: int)`)
- `invocation()` returns the **caller's** invocation — inherited from
  whatever action/hook/function called `apply_condition()` or
  `remove_condition()`. No fresh invocation is minted for lifecycle blocks.

## Inheritance via extends

If `condition Stunned extends Incapacitated on bearer: Character` and
both have lifecycle blocks:

- **on_apply**: ancestor blocks run first (DFS post-order, matching
  modify clause ordering). Incapacitated.on_apply then Stunned.on_apply.
- **on_remove**: same ancestor-first ordering.
  Incapacitated.on_remove then Stunned.on_remove.

This matches the existing modify clause inheritance behavior.

## Error Semantics

**on_apply error**: the condition is **rolled back** (removed without
firing on_remove). The apply did not succeed. The error propagates
to the caller of `apply_condition()`.

**on_remove error**: the condition is **still removed** (removal must
succeed for state consistency). The error is propagated but does not
prevent removal.

## Implementation Sketch

### AST changes (ttrpg_ast)

Add two new variants to `ConditionClause`:
```rust
pub enum ConditionClause {
    Modify(ModifyClause),
    Suppress(SuppressClause),
    OnApply(LifecycleBlock),   // new
    OnRemove(LifecycleBlock),  // new
}

pub struct LifecycleBlock {
    pub body: Vec<Stmt>,
    pub span: Span,
}
```

### Parser changes (ttrpg_parser)

Inside condition body parsing, recognize `on_apply` and `on_remove`
keywords followed by a block. Enforce at-most-one of each (parser
error on duplicates).

### Checker changes (ttrpg_checker)

- Type-check lifecycle block bodies in a scope containing `bearer` +
  condition params.
- **Enforce restrictions**: error on `apply_condition`, `remove_condition`,
  `revoke(invocation)` calls inside lifecycle blocks. Allow `revoke` on
  groups (syntactically distinct: `revoke entity.Group` vs `revoke(expr)`).
- Validate inherited lifecycle blocks from `extends` ancestors.

### Interpreter changes (ttrpg_interp)

- **apply_condition path**: after inserting the ActiveCondition into
  entity state, collect on_apply blocks (ancestor chain, DFS post-order),
  execute each. On error, remove the condition (no on_remove) and
  propagate error.
- **remove_condition path**: before removing the ActiveCondition,
  collect on_remove blocks (ancestor chain, DFS post-order), execute
  each. Remove condition regardless of on_remove errors.
- Runtime flag `in_lifecycle_block: bool` on execution environment to
  enforce restrictions dynamically as a safety net.

## Deferred to Phase 2

- **on_tick**: requires resolving game_time vs turn-relative timing,
  tick frequency, and ordering across entities
- **Auto-expiry**: duration-based automatic condition removal
- **Condition state**: mutable fields on conditions (if a use case
  emerges that needs persistence between lifecycle blocks)"}

{"design":"# Condition Lifecycle Hooks: on_apply / on_remove (Phase 1)

> on_tick is deferred to a second design pass — it requires resolving
> game_time vs turn-relative timing semantics first.

## Motivation

Conditions are purely declarative today (modify + suppress). They cannot
execute imperative code when applied or removed. This blocks use cases like:
- Dropping held items when Unconscious is applied
- Breaking concentration when Stunned is applied
- Emitting status change events for UI/logging
- Cleaning up entity state when a condition wears off

Current workaround — orphan hooks that check `conditions(entity)` on
turn_start — separates behavior from its definition and requires the host
to fire the right events.

## Design Principle: extends over apply_condition

Conditions that imply other conditions should use `extends`, not
imperative `apply_condition()` inside on_apply:

```
// GOOD: declarative relationship
condition Unconscious extends Incapacitated, Prone on bearer: Character { ... }

// BAD: imperative sub-condition management
condition Unconscious on bearer: Character {
    on_apply { apply_condition(bearer, Prone, indefinite) }  // forbidden
}
```

`extends` gives automatic inheritance of modify/suppress clauses and
automatic cleanup on removal. No invocation ownership questions.

## Syntax

New `ConditionClause` variants alongside `Modify` and `Suppress`.
At most one `on_apply` and one `on_remove` per condition declaration.

```
condition Burning(damage: int) on bearer: Character {
    on_apply {
        emit StatusGained(target: bearer, status: "Burning")
        bearer.took_fire_damage = true
    }

    on_remove {
        emit StatusLost(target: bearer, status: "Burning")
    }

    modify fire_resistance(target: bearer) { ... }
    suppress regeneration(target: bearer)
}
```

## Trigger Points

**on_apply** fires immediately **before** the condition is activated.
The condition is not yet active — its modify/suppress clauses are NOT
in effect during on_apply execution. If on_apply succeeds, the condition
activates. If on_apply errors, the condition is never applied (no
rollback needed since it was never active).

**on_remove** fires immediately before the condition is stripped.
The condition's modify/suppress clauses are still in effect during
on_remove execution. Fires on:
- `remove_condition()` — explicit removal. When a single call matches
  multiple instances, on_remove fires **once per instance** in
  `gained_at` order (oldest first). All matched instances are removed
  regardless of individual on_remove errors; the first error is
  propagated after all removals complete.
- `revoke(invocation)` — bulk removal by invocation. The interpreter
  expands this into per-instance removals: each matching condition's
  on_remove fires individually, in `gained_at` order.
- Future auto-expiry (when on_tick is implemented)

## Capabilities

Lifecycle blocks have **hook-like** execution semantics:
- Can mutate entity state
- Can emit events
- Can roll dice
- Can call derives, mechanics, functions

## Restrictions (enforced)

The following are **directly forbidden** inside on_apply / on_remove blocks:
- `apply_condition()` — prevents reentrancy and condition graph antipattern
- `remove_condition()` — same reasoning
- `revoke(invocation)` — removes conditions, same restriction
- `invocation()` — no meaningful use since condition manipulation is
  forbidden; avoids ambiguity when caller has no active invocation

`revoke entity.Group` (optional group revocation) is **allowed** — that
manipulates entity structure, not conditions.

These restrictions are enforced at the checker level (static analysis)
for direct calls, with a runtime guard (`in_lifecycle_block` flag) as
fallback.

**Indirect bypass via emit**: a lifecycle block may emit an event whose
hook calls `apply_condition()`. This is **not transitively prevented** —
transitive enforcement would require tracing all reachable code paths
and would make `emit` unusable in lifecycle blocks. This is documented
as a discouraged pattern; the direct restriction catches the primary
antipattern (conditions managing condition graphs).

Rationale: if a use case arises that genuinely needs condition
manipulation inside lifecycle blocks, we can lift the restriction then
with a concrete motivating example to design around.

## Scoping

- `bearer` (the receiver) is in scope
- Condition parameters are in scope (e.g., `damage` for `Burning(damage: int)`)
- `invocation()` is **not available** (checker error)

## Inheritance via extends

If `condition Stunned extends Incapacitated on bearer: Character` and
both have lifecycle blocks:

- **on_apply**: ancestor blocks run first (DFS post-order, matching
  modify clause ordering). Incapacitated.on_apply then Stunned.on_apply.
  If an ancestor's on_apply errors, descendant on_apply blocks do not
  run and the condition is not applied.
- **on_remove**: same ancestor-first ordering.
  Incapacitated.on_remove then Stunned.on_remove.

This matches the existing modify clause inheritance behavior.

## Error Semantics

**on_apply error**: the condition is **never activated** (it fired
before activation, so there is nothing to roll back). Side effects
from the partial on_apply execution (mutations, emits) are not undone —
they are fire-and-forget, consistent with hook error semantics. The
error propagates to the caller of `apply_condition()`.

**on_remove error**: the condition is **still removed** (removal must
succeed for state consistency). The error is propagated but does not
prevent removal. When multiple instances are being removed, remaining
on_remove blocks still execute.

## Implementation Sketch

### AST changes (ttrpg_ast)

Add two new variants to `ConditionClause`:
```rust
pub enum ConditionClause {
    Modify(ModifyClause),
    Suppress(SuppressClause),
    OnApply(LifecycleBlock),   // new
    OnRemove(LifecycleBlock),  // new
}

pub struct LifecycleBlock {
    pub body: Vec<Stmt>,
    pub span: Span,
}
```

### Parser changes (ttrpg_parser)

Inside condition body parsing, recognize `on_apply` and `on_remove`
keywords followed by a block. Enforce at-most-one of each (parser
error on duplicates).

### Checker changes (ttrpg_checker)

- Type-check lifecycle block bodies in a scope containing `bearer` +
  condition params.
- **Enforce restrictions**: error on `apply_condition`, `remove_condition`,
  `revoke(invocation)`, and `invocation()` calls inside lifecycle blocks.
  Allow `revoke` on groups (syntactically distinct: `revoke entity.Group`
  vs `revoke(expr)`).
- Validate inherited lifecycle blocks from `extends` ancestors.

### Interpreter changes (ttrpg_interp)

- **apply_condition path**: collect on_apply blocks (ancestor chain,
  DFS post-order), execute each BEFORE inserting the ActiveCondition.
  On success, insert and activate. On error, do not insert; propagate.
- **remove_condition path**: expand to per-instance iteration in
  `gained_at` order. For each instance: collect on_remove blocks
  (ancestor chain, DFS post-order), execute each, then remove the
  instance. Continue regardless of on_remove errors; propagate first
  error after all removals complete.
- **revoke(invocation) path**: expand bulk removal into per-instance
  removals following the same on_remove protocol above.
- Runtime flag `in_lifecycle_block: bool` on execution environment to
  enforce restrictions dynamically as a safety net.

## Test Plan

1. **Partial failure in on_apply**: verify that mutations/emits from a
   failing on_apply are preserved (fire-and-forget) but the condition
   is never activated.
2. **revoke path**: verify per-instance on_remove execution in
   deterministic `gained_at` order when `revoke(invocation)` removes
   multiple conditions.
3. **Nested emit from lifecycle block**: verify that a hook triggered
   by emit inside on_apply CAN call `apply_condition` (indirect bypass
   is allowed), confirming restrictions are direct-only.
4. **Inherited lifecycle**: verify ancestor on_apply/on_remove blocks
   execute in correct DFS post-order.
5. **Multi-match remove_condition**: verify on_remove fires per instance
   and all instances are removed even if one on_remove errors.
6. **invocation() in lifecycle block**: verify checker error.

## Deferred to Phase 2

- **on_tick**: requires resolving game_time vs turn-relative timing,
  tick frequency, and ordering across entities
- **Auto-expiry**: duration-based automatic condition removal
- **Condition state**: mutable fields on conditions (if a use case
  emerges that needs persistence between lifecycle blocks)"}

{"design":"## Design: Tags on Modify Clauses

### Syntax

Tags on modify clauses — after bindings, before body (consistent with action/function tag placement):

```
condition Prone on bearer: entity {
    modify [#attack_resolution](target: bearer) #position #penalty {
        attack_mod = attack_mod + 4
    }
}
```

### Suppress-Modify via Selector

New suppress variant using `[selector]` syntax (disambiguated from event suppress by leading `[`):

```
condition FreedomOfMovement on bearer: entity {
    suppress [#position](target: bearer)
}
```

- Supports full selector predicates: `#tag`, `returns Type`, `has param`
- Bindings are optional — omitting them suppresses all matching modify clauses regardless of entity
- Self-suppression is allowed (universal application, user error if contradictory)

### Semantics

**Suppress-modify evaluation** (in collect_modifiers_owned):
1. Collect all active suppress-modify clauses from entities in scope
2. For each candidate modifier, check if any suppress-modify clause matches by (a) tag/selector overlap and (b) binding match
3. Filter out suppressed modifiers before running phase 1/phase 2

**Binding match**: `suppress [#position](target: bearer)` means suppress any modify clause tagged #position where a binding named `target` resolves to this condition's bearer.

**Ordering**: Gained-order applies. Later-gained suppress overrides earlier-gained modifiers. "Un-suppress" (suppress a suppress) is out of scope for v0.

### Provenance

- `Effect::ModifyApplied` extended with modify clause tags
- REPL `breakdown` command shows per-modifier provenance: condition → tags → param/result changes → values
- Suppressed modifiers shown annotated (not omitted) for debugging

### AST Changes

- `ModifyClause`: add `tags: Vec<Name>`
- New `ConditionClause::SuppressModify` variant (separate from event suppress for clarity)

### Cost-modify tags deferred

Tags on `modify X.cost(...)` clauses are out of scope — separate concern with own binding semantics.

### Implementation Phases

1. AST: Add `tags: Vec<Name>` to `ModifyClause`
2. Parser: Parse `#tag` after modify bindings, before `{`
3. Checker: Validate modify clause tags exist
4. Interpreter: Include tags in `Effect::ModifyApplied`
5. AST: New `SuppressModify` variant
6. Parser: Parse `suppress [selector](bindings)`
7. Checker: Validate suppress-modify clauses
8. Interpreter: Filter suppressed modifiers in `collect_modifiers_owned`
9. REPL: `breakdown` command
10. Docs: Update spec and documentation"}

{"id":"tdsl-a5y0","title":"Design: resource threshold triggers","description":"No way to automatically react when a resource crosses a boundary. Currently threshold checks are manual in every action that modifies HP (TakeDamage checks hp<=0). If HP is modified any other way (healing overflow, direct assignment, level drain), checks are missed. Two design options: (A) on-threshold syntax on entity resource fields: 'on hp <= 0 { ... }', or (B) auto-emit ResourceChanged event whenever interpreter mutates a resource, letting hooks react. Option B is lighter-weight and more composable. Blocks: auto-unconscious at 0 HP, auto-death at -10 HP, exhaustion thresholds, spell slot depletion triggers.","design":"## Design: restricted field modifier

### Summary
Add a `restricted` keyword modifier on entity/group fields that prevents mutation from outside the declaring module (system block). Read access is always allowed. This enforces encapsulation — resource mutations must go through actions exported by the declaring module.

### Syntax
```
entity Character {
    restricted HP: resource(0..=max_HP)
    restricted spell_slots: map<int, resource(0..=9)>
    name: string  // unrestricted, anyone can mutate
}

group HasHP {
    restricted hp: resource(0..=max_hp)
    max_hp: int
}
```

### Semantics
- `restricted` is opt-in per field (default: unrestricted)
- Applies to any entity or group field, not just resources
- **Declaring module** = the `system "Name" { ... }` block containing the field declaration
- Multiple `system "Name"` blocks with same name merge — all share write access
- Read access is always allowed from any module
- Assignment (`=`, `+=`, `-=`) to a restricted field is a **checker error** if the assignment statement is outside the declaring module

### Exceptions
- `grant entity.Group { field_inits }` is allowed cross-module (initialization, not ongoing mutation)
- `revoke entity.Group` is allowed cross-module (removes entire group, not field-level mutation)
- REPL direct assignment (`set`) bypasses restriction (debug tool)

### Module ownership for groups
If system A declares a group with a restricted field, and system B includes that group on an entity, **system A owns the restriction**. System B must call actions from system A to mutate the field. This is intentional: the group author controls the mutation contract.

### Error message
```
error: cannot mutate restricted field `HP` outside declaring module
  --> spells.tdsl:42:9
   |
42 |     target.HP = 0
   |     ^^^^^^^^^^^^ mutation not allowed here
   |
   = note: `HP` is declared as restricted in system "Core Combat"
   = help: use an action from "Core Combat" to modify this field
```

### Audit result
No cross-module mutations exist in the current codebase. This feature is forward-looking protection with zero migration burden.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"feature","owner":"tfgast@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-03-04T06:00:14Z","created_by":"Theodore Gast","updated_at":"2026-03-04T15:49:23Z"}

new_value:
{"description":"Restructure OSRIC Character entity to use restricted groups for field encapsulation.

## Plan

### 1. EquipmentSlots group (OSRIC Equipment)
- Move wielded_main, wielded_off, worn_armor, worn_shield, current_weight into an included group EquipmentSlots declared in OSRIC Equipment
- Mark all fields restricted
- Invariant: current_weight must stay in sync with equipped items via equip/unequip functions
- Access: included, so reads like char.wielded_main still work

### 2. HitPoints group (OSRIC Combat)
- Move hp and max_hp into an included group HitPoints declared in OSRIC Combat
- Mark hp restricted (max_hp stays unrestricted)
- Invariant: damage must flow through TakeDamage for events/reduction/death checks
- Access: included, so reads like char.hp still work

### 3. Spellcasting group (OSRIC Spells)
- Move Spellcasting optional group declaration from OSRIC core to OSRIC Spells
- Mark memorised_spells, slots_used, casting_invocation restricted
- Move BeginCasting action from OSRIC Initiative to OSRIC Spells (casting logic belongs with spells)
- Invariant: slots_used syncs with memorised_spells; casting_invocation tracks active state
- spell_slots stays unrestricted (set during character creation)

## Implementation order
1. EquipmentSlots group
2. HitPoints group
3. Spellcasting group + move BeginCasting"}

{"id":"tdsl-k6nj","title":"Add tags on modify clauses for provenance and targeted suppression","description":"Add support for tagging modify clauses within conditions, enabling two capabilities:

1. **Targeted suppression by category**: Conditions can suppress entire categories of modifiers rather than needing to know specific condition names. E.g., `suppress [#position](target: bearer)` removes all position-based penalties, `suppress [#fear](target: bearer)` removes all fear effects.

2. **Audit trail / provenance**: The REPL can show a breakdown of where numeric modifiers come from, grouped by tag. E.g., `breakdown attack_roll(target: Thog)` shows each modifier with its tag, source condition, and value.

**Syntax sketch:**
```
condition Prone on bearer: entity {
    modify [#attack_resolution](target: bearer) #position #penalty {
        attack_mod = attack_mod + 4
    }
}

condition FreedomOfMovement on bearer: entity {
    suppress [#position](target: bearer)
}
```

**Scope:**
- Parser: allow tags after modify clause bindings, before body
- AST: store tags on ModifyClause
- Checker: validate tags exist
- Interpreter: filter tagged modify clauses during suppress evaluation
- REPL: add breakdown command showing provenance by tag

**Not in scope:** Removing boolean params from resolve_melee_attack (that is better solved by decomposing mechanics into sub-functions). Also not in scope: bonus type stacking rules (keep the DSL system-agnostic).","design":"## Design: Tags on Modify Clauses

### Syntax

Tags on modify clauses — after bindings, before body (consistent with action/function tag placement):

```
condition Prone on bearer: entity {
    modify [#attack_resolution](target: bearer) #position #penalty {
        attack_mod = attack_mod + 4
    }
}
```

### Suppress-Modify via Selector

New suppress variant using `[selector]` syntax (disambiguated from event suppress by leading `[`):

```
condition FreedomOfMovement on bearer: entity {
    suppress [#position](target: bearer)
}
```

- Supports full selector predicates: `#tag`, `returns Type`, `has param`
- Bindings are optional — omitting them suppresses all matching modify clauses regardless of entity
- Self-suppression is allowed (universal application, user error if contradictory)

### Semantics

**Suppress-modify evaluation** (in collect_modifiers_owned):
1. Collect all active suppress-modify clauses from entities in scope
2. For each candidate modifier, check if any suppress-modify clause matches by (a) tag/selector overlap and (b) binding match
3. Filter out suppressed modifiers before running phase 1/phase 2

**Binding match**: `suppress [#position](target: bearer)` means suppress any modify clause tagged #position where a binding named `target` resolves to this condition's bearer.

**Ordering**: Gained-order applies. Later-gained suppress overrides earlier-gained modifiers. "Un-suppress" (suppress a suppress) is out of scope for v0.

### Provenance

- `Effect::ModifyApplied` extended with modify clause tags
- REPL `breakdown` command shows per-modifier provenance: condition → tags → param/result changes → values
- Suppressed modifiers shown annotated (not omitted) for debugging

### AST Changes

- `ModifyClause`: add `tags: Vec<Name>`
- New `ConditionClause::SuppressModify` variant (separate from event suppress for clarity)

### Cost-modify tags deferred

Tags on `modify X.cost(...)` clauses are out of scope — separate concern with own binding semantics.

### Implementation Phases

1. AST: Add `tags: Vec<Name>` to `ModifyClause`
2. Parser: Parse `#tag` after modify bindings, before `{`
3. Checker: Validate modify clause tags exist
4. Interpreter: Include tags in `Effect::ModifyApplied`
5. AST: New `SuppressModify` variant
6. Parser: Parse `suppress [selector](bindings)`
7. Checker: Validate suppress-modify clauses
8. Interpreter: Filter suppressed modifiers in `collect_modifiers_owned`
9. REPL: `breakdown` command
10. Docs: Update spec and documentation","status":"open","priority":3,"issue_type":"feature","owner":"tfgast@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-03-03T04:42:42Z","created_by":"Theodore Gast","updated_at":"2026-03-05T04:30:12Z"}

{"id":"tdsl-eqet","title":"Design: condition lifecycle hooks (on_apply/on_remove) — Phase 1","description":"Conditions are purely declarative today (modify + suppress). They cannot execute code when applied, removed, or each round. This blocks: bleeding at 0 HP (1 HP/round loss), poison over time, ongoing spell effects (Wall of Fire damage each round), condition side-effects (Unconscious should also apply Prone/drop items). Current workaround (orphan hooks on RoundStart that check for conditions) separates behavior from definition and only works inside resolve_round(). Proposed syntax: on_apply/on_tick/on_remove blocks inside condition declarations. Needs design doc covering: syntax, scoping rules (can on_tick mutate? emit?), tick trigger mechanism (who calls tick — interpreter or DSL event?), interaction with duration/expiry.","design":"# Condition Lifecycle Hooks: on_apply / on_remove (Phase 1)

> on_tick is deferred to a second design pass — it requires resolving
> game_time vs turn-relative timing semantics first.

## Motivation

Conditions are purely declarative today (modify + suppress). They cannot
execute imperative code when applied or removed. This blocks use cases like:
- Dropping held items when Unconscious is applied
- Breaking concentration when Stunned is applied
- Emitting status change events for UI/logging
- Cleaning up entity state when a condition wears off

Current workaround — orphan hooks that check `conditions(entity)` on
turn_start — separates behavior from its definition and requires the host
to fire the right events.

## Design Principle: extends over apply_condition

Conditions that imply other conditions should use `extends`, not
imperative `apply_condition()` inside on_apply:

```
// GOOD: declarative relationship
condition Unconscious extends Incapacitated, Prone on bearer: Character { ... }

// BAD: imperative sub-condition management
condition Unconscious on bearer: Character {
    on_apply { apply_condition(bearer, Prone, indefinite) }  // forbidden
}
```

`extends` gives automatic inheritance of modify/suppress clauses and
automatic cleanup on removal. No invocation ownership questions.

## Syntax

New `ConditionClause` variants alongside `Modify` and `Suppress`.
At most one `on_apply` and one `on_remove` per condition declaration.

```
condition Burning(damage: int) on bearer: Character {
    on_apply {
        emit StatusGained(target: bearer, status: "Burning")
        bearer.took_fire_damage = true
    }

    on_remove {
        emit StatusLost(target: bearer, status: "Burning")
    }

    modify fire_resistance(target: bearer) { ... }
    suppress regeneration(target: bearer)
}
```

## Trigger Points

**on_apply** fires immediately **before** the condition is activated.
The condition is not yet active — its modify/suppress clauses are NOT
in effect during on_apply execution. If on_apply succeeds, the condition
activates. If on_apply errors, the condition is never applied (no
rollback needed since it was never active).

**on_remove** fires immediately before the condition is stripped.
The condition's modify/suppress clauses are still in effect during
on_remove execution. Fires on:
- `remove_condition()` — explicit removal. When a single call matches
  multiple instances, on_remove fires **once per instance** in
  `gained_at` order (oldest first). All matched instances are removed
  regardless of individual on_remove errors; the first error is
  propagated after all removals complete.
- `revoke(invocation)` — bulk removal by invocation. The interpreter
  expands this into per-instance removals: each matching condition's
  on_remove fires individually, in `gained_at` order.
- Future auto-expiry (when on_tick is implemented)

## Capabilities

Lifecycle blocks have **hook-like** execution semantics:
- Can mutate entity state
- Can emit events
- Can roll dice
- Can call derives, mechanics, functions

## Restrictions (enforced)

The following are **directly forbidden** inside on_apply / on_remove blocks:
- `apply_condition()` — prevents reentrancy and condition graph antipattern
- `remove_condition()` — same reasoning
- `revoke(invocation)` — removes conditions, same restriction
- `invocation()` — no meaningful use since condition manipulation is
  forbidden; avoids ambiguity when caller has no active invocation

`revoke entity.Group` (optional group revocation) is **allowed** — that
manipulates entity structure, not conditions.

These restrictions are enforced at the checker level (static analysis)
for direct calls, with a runtime guard (`in_lifecycle_block` flag) as
fallback.

**Indirect bypass via emit**: a lifecycle block may emit an event whose
hook calls `apply_condition()`. This is **not transitively prevented** —
transitive enforcement would require tracing all reachable code paths
and would make `emit` unusable in lifecycle blocks. This is documented
as a discouraged pattern; the direct restriction catches the primary
antipattern (conditions managing condition graphs).

Rationale: if a use case arises that genuinely needs condition
manipulation inside lifecycle blocks, we can lift the restriction then
with a concrete motivating example to design around.

## Scoping

- `bearer` (the receiver) is in scope
- Condition parameters are in scope (e.g., `damage` for `Burning(damage: int)`)
- `invocation()` is **not available** (checker error)

## Inheritance via extends

If `condition Stunned extends Incapacitated on bearer: Character` and
both have lifecycle blocks:

- **on_apply**: ancestor blocks run first (DFS post-order, matching
  modify clause ordering). Incapacitated.on_apply then Stunned.on_apply.
  If an ancestor's on_apply errors, descendant on_apply blocks do not
  run and the condition is not applied.
- **on_remove**: same ancestor-first ordering.
  Incapacitated.on_remove then Stunned.on_remove.

This matches the existing modify clause inheritance behavior.

## Error Semantics

**on_apply error**: the condition is **never activated** (it fired
before activation, so there is nothing to roll back). Side effects
from the partial on_apply execution (mutations, emits) are not undone —
they are fire-and-forget, consistent with hook error semantics. The
error propagates to the caller of `apply_condition()`.

**on_remove error**: the condition is **still removed** (removal must
succeed for state consistency). The error is propagated but does not
prevent removal. When multiple instances are being removed, remaining
on_remove blocks still execute.

## Implementation Sketch

### AST changes (ttrpg_ast)

Add two new variants to `ConditionClause`:
```rust
pub enum ConditionClause {
    Modify(ModifyClause),
    Suppress(SuppressClause),
    OnApply(LifecycleBlock),   // new
    OnRemove(LifecycleBlock),  // new
}

pub struct LifecycleBlock {
    pub body: Vec<Stmt>,
    pub span: Span,
}
```

### Parser changes (ttrpg_parser)

Inside condition body parsing, recognize `on_apply` and `on_remove`
keywords followed by a block. Enforce at-most-one of each (parser
error on duplicates).

### Checker changes (ttrpg_checker)

- Type-check lifecycle block bodies in a scope containing `bearer` +
  condition params.
- **Enforce restrictions**: error on `apply_condition`, `remove_condition`,
  `revoke(invocation)`, and `invocation()` calls inside lifecycle blocks.
  Allow `revoke` on groups (syntactically distinct: `revoke entity.Group`
  vs `revoke(expr)`).
- Validate inherited lifecycle blocks from `extends` ancestors.

### Interpreter changes (ttrpg_interp)

- **apply_condition path**: collect on_apply blocks (ancestor chain,
  DFS post-order), execute each BEFORE inserting the ActiveCondition.
  On success, insert and activate. On error, do not insert; propagate.
- **remove_condition path**: expand to per-instance iteration in
  `gained_at` order. For each instance: collect on_remove blocks
  (ancestor chain, DFS post-order), execute each, then remove the
  instance. Continue regardless of on_remove errors; propagate first
  error after all removals complete.
- **revoke(invocation) path**: expand bulk removal into per-instance
  removals following the same on_remove protocol above.
- Runtime flag `in_lifecycle_block: bool` on execution environment to
  enforce restrictions dynamically as a safety net.

## Test Plan

1. **Partial failure in on_apply**: verify that mutations/emits from a
   failing on_apply are preserved (fire-and-forget) but the condition
   is never activated.
2. **revoke path**: verify per-instance on_remove execution in
   deterministic `gained_at` order when `revoke(invocation)` removes
   multiple conditions.
3. **Nested emit from lifecycle block**: verify that a hook triggered
   by emit inside on_apply CAN call `apply_condition` (indirect bypass
   is allowed), confirming restrictions are direct-only.
4. **Inherited lifecycle**: verify ancestor on_apply/on_remove blocks
   execute in correct DFS post-order.
5. **Multi-match remove_condition**: verify on_remove fires per instance
   and all instances are removed even if one on_remove errors.
6. **invocation() in lifecycle block**: verify checker error.

## Deferred to Phase 2

- **on_tick**: requires resolving game_time vs turn-relative timing,
  tick frequency, and ordering across entities
- **Auto-expiry**: duration-based automatic condition removal
- **Condition state**: mutable fields on conditions (if a use case
  emerges that needs persistence between lifecycle blocks)","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"feature","owner":"tfgast@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-03-04T06:00:08Z","created_by":"Theodore Gast","updated_at":"2026-03-05T07:19:29Z","closed_at":"2026-03-05T07:19:29Z","close_reason":"Closed"}

Condition state deferred until a concrete use case emerges."}

{"design":"## Level Drain Design

### Rules (§1.6.7)
- Lose one level of experience, XP set to beginning of new lower level
- Multi/dual-class: drain highest level; random (roll) if tied
- Below level 1 = death (PCs)
- **Restorable** by high-level magic (restoration) — data must support undo

### Key Decisions
- **Pure derives** for logic, applied one level at a time (caller loops for multi-drain)
- **Randomness**: use roll() for tied-class selection; testable via roll specs
- **HP recalc**: deferred to tdsl-1xcs
- **NPC rules**: deferred to tdsl-cnww
- **Restorability**: drain result must preserve enough info to reverse (or restoration simply sets XP/level back)

### Derives
1. highest_class_level(char) -> int
2. drain_candidate_classes(char) -> list<Class>  // classes at max level
3. drain_target_class(char) -> Class  // picks from candidates, rolls if tied
4. xp_after_drain(class, current_level) -> int  // xp_for_level(class, level-1)
5. apply_level_drain(classes: list<ClassLevel>, target: Class) -> list<ClassLevel>
6. is_killed_by_drain(level: int) -> bool  // level < 1

### File: osric/osric_level_drain.ttrpg
### Test: crates/ttrpg_interp/tests/osric_level_drain_integration.rs"}

{"design":"## Level Drain Design

### Rules (§1.6.7, §1.8.2.7)
- Lose one level of experience, XP set to beginning of new lower level
- Multi/dual-class: drain highest level; random (roll) if tied
- Below level 1 = death (PCs)
- **Restorable** via Restoration (Cleric 7): restores one lost level, XP to start of restored level
- Restoration time window: 1 day per caster's cleric level. Miss it and the level is gone permanently.
- Drain and restore are symmetric — no need to track old XP, just the event

### Data Model (in osric_core.ttrpg)

```
struct DrainEvent {
    class: Class         // which class was drained
    original_level: int  // level before this drain
    timestamp: int       // game-time in segments when drain occurred
}
```

Character entity gets: `drain_history: list<DrainEvent>`

### Key Decisions
- **Randomness**: use roll() for tied-class selection; testable via roll specs
- **One drain at a time**: caller loops for multi-drain undead
- **Restore order**: oldest applicable drain first (FIFO) — already punishing enough
- **Time unit**: segments (existing helpers convert days/turns/rounds)
- **HP recalc**: deferred to tdsl-1xcs
- **NPC rules**: deferred to tdsl-cnww

### Derives (in osric/osric_level_drain.ttrpg)
1. highest_class_level(char) -> int
2. drain_candidate_classes(char) -> list<Class>
3. drain_target_class(char) -> Class  // rolls if tied
4. xp_after_drain(class, current_level) -> int
5. apply_level_drain(classes: list<ClassLevel>, target: Class) -> list<ClassLevel>
6. is_killed_by_drain(level: int) -> bool
7. record_drain_event(char, target_class, current_time) -> list<DrainEvent>
8. restoration_eligible(drain: DrainEvent, current_time: int, caster_cleric_level: int) -> bool
9. apply_restoration(char, current_time, caster_cleric_level) -> list<ClassLevel>  // restores oldest eligible
10. remove_oldest_eligible_drain(drain_history, current_time, caster_cleric_level) -> list<DrainEvent>

### Files
- osric/osric_core.ttrpg — DrainEvent struct, drain_history on Character
- osric/osric_level_drain.ttrpg — all derives
- crates/ttrpg_interp/tests/osric_level_drain_integration.rs — tests"}

{"design":"## Design Decisions (2026-03-05)

### File organization
Split into two files: `osric_cleric_spells.ttrpg` (Bless/Curse, CLW/CLW-reverse, Hold Person) and `osric_mu_spells.ttrpg` (Sleep, Magic Missile, Fireball).

### Pattern per spell
1. `derive <spell>_def() -> SpellDef` — static data
2. `function resolve_<spell>(...)` — effect resolution
3. Per-spell integration test in `crates/ttrpg_interp/tests/`

### New conditions
- `Blessed` (+1 attack, +5% morale) — 6 rounds duration
- `Cursed` (-1 attack, -5% morale) — 6 rounds duration
Hold Person reuses `Paralyzed`, Sleep reuses `Sleeping`.

### Caster level derive
`caster_level(char, spell_class) -> int` in `osric_class.ttrpg`.

### Reverse spells
Implement Curse and Cause Light Wounds alongside their forward versions.

### Damage events
Spell damage uses existing `Damaged(target, attacker, amount)` event to trigger spell interruption hooks.

### Sleep
Full HD-category fidelity (per-category rolls, weakest-first processing). Separate bead due to complexity.

### Implementation order
Bless/Curse → CLW/CLW-rev → Magic Missile → Fireball → Hold Person → Sleep"}

old_value: {"id":"tdsl-8woy","title":"Support covariant list types in the checker","description":"The type checker enforces exact type matching for generic containers like list\u003cT\u003e. A list\u003cCharacter\u003e cannot be passed where list\u003centity\u003e is expected, even though Character is an entity type. This forced resolve_bless to change from list\u003centity\u003e to list\u003cCharacter\u003e (commit b7be1c2) to satisfy the spell registry dispatch. Adding covariance for list/set types would allow passing list\u003cSubType\u003e where list\u003cSuperType\u003e is expected, matching structural subtyping expectations.","status":"open","priority":3,"issue_type":"feature","owner":"tfgast@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-03-05T23:34:58Z","created_by":"Theodore Gast","updated_at":"2026-03-05T23:34:58Z"}

old_value: {"id":"tdsl-pljd","title":"Implement type-based action dispatch (receiver overloading)","description":"Allow multiple action declarations with the same name but different receiver types (e.g., TakeDamage on Character vs TakeDamage on Monster). The checker validates each overload independently. At call sites: if receiver type is known statically, resolve to the matching overload; if receiver is generic entity, require all overloads have compatible param signatures and emit runtime dispatch. Changes needed: AST (Program::actions HashMap→Vec or composite key), checker (collect.rs allow multiple, check_call.rs overload resolution), interpreter (dispatch_action select by entity type at runtime).","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"feature","owner":"tfgast@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-03-06T00:19:13Z","created_by":"Theodore Gast","updated_at":"2026-03-06T00:19:13Z"}

new_value: Implemented type-based action dispatch. Actions can now be overloaded by receiver type. Resolution: exact entity type match > generic entity fallback > single overload.

old_value: {"id":"tdsl-iaa4","title":"Unify mixed entity types to AnyEntity in list literals","description":"List literals like [monster, character] fail the checker because unify_branch_types doesn't widen Entity(A) + Entity(B) to AnyEntity. Add a case: if both sides are entity types, return AnyEntity. This is safe because AnyEntity is already fully supported in field access, has-checks, modify blocks, and the interpreter. Note: heterogeneous lists can already be built via .append() on a list\u003centity\u003e, so this is just a gap in literal inference.","status":"open","priority":3,"issue_type":"feature","owner":"tfgast@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-03-06T00:29:09Z","created_by":"Theodore Gast","updated_at":"2026-03-06T00:29:09Z"}

old_value: {"id":"tdsl-peey","title":"No effective_target_ac overload for Monster targets","description":"effective_target_ac(target: Character, ...) at osric_combat.ttrpg:319 has no Monster overload. Monsters have an ac: int field but there is no way to compute effective AC when a Monster is the target of an attack.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","owner":"tfgast@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-03-06T05:49:48Z","created_by":"Theodore Gast","updated_at":"2026-03-06T05:49:48Z"}

{"id":"tdsl-p3in","title":"Add 'is Entity' type narrowing for entity parameters","description":"Currently entity parameters can only be narrowed via 'has Group' guards. Add 'target is Character' / 'target is Monster' syntax that narrows the type within the if-branch, allowing field access on the concrete entity type. This would enable writing single polymorphic functions that branch on entity type without needing separate Character/Monster wrappers. Motivation: spell functions like make_saving_throw need different field access per entity type (classes vs hit_dice). Without type narrowing, we need separate functions and manual dispatch.","status":"open","priority":3,"issue_type":"feature","owner":"tfgast@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-03-06T00:01:01Z","created_by":"Theodore Gast","updated_at":"2026-03-06T00:01:01Z"}

new_value: Implemented is-entity type narrowing across AST, parser, checker, and interpreter
Added SpellTestContext builder to osric_common and refactored all 3 spell test files to use it
Changed effective_target_ac to accept entity type with is-narrowing: Characters use full armor/DEX/shield calculation, Monsters use flat ac field

old_value: {"id":"tdsl-ud4e","title":"Monster combat action overloads missing","description":"All main attack actions (MeleeAttack, MissileAttack, TwoWeaponAttack, Charge, SetWeaponStrike, Backstab) only accept Character as attacker. A resolve_monster_attack() mechanic exists at osric_combat.ttrpg:446 but is never wrapped in action overloads. Monsters cannot attack through the action pipeline. Need Monster overloads for at least MeleeAttack and MissileAttack.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","owner":"tfgast@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-03-06T05:49:44Z","created_by":"Theodore Gast","updated_at":"2026-03-06T05:49:44Z"}

new_value: Added Monster MeleeAttack and MissileAttack action overloads using resolve_monster_attack. Also widened resolve_monster_attack target to entity.

old_value: {"id":"tdsl-ktdj","title":"REPL: bare enum variants fail when not unique_variant_owner","description":"In CLI eval expressions, bare enum variant names like Cleric or MagicUser fail with 'undefined variable' when the variant name isn't uniquely owned by a single enum (unique_variant_owner returns None). Qualified syntax Class.Cleric works. The eval_ident fallback in eval/dispatch.rs:295-301 relies on unique_variant_owner which only resolves if the name appears in exactly one enum. Should resolve unambiguous variants in the context of the function call's expected parameter type.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","owner":"tfgast@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-03-06T07:03:41Z","created_by":"Theodore Gast","updated_at":"2026-03-06T07:03:41Z"}

new_value: Added type-hint-based enum variant resolution in bind_args for REPL eval expressions

old_value: {"id":"tdsl-3htm","title":"REPL: qualified enum variant with struct body fails in expressions","description":"WieldedItem.Melee { weapon: SwordLong } fails with 'unexpected trailing token: LBrace' in eval expressions. The call-style WieldedItem.Melee(SwordLong) works as a workaround. The expression parser handles Melee { weapon: SwordLong } (bare variant + struct body) but not the qualified Enum.Variant { fields } form. This matters for set commands and assert_eq expressions that need to construct enum variants with fields.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","owner":"tfgast@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-03-06T07:03:41Z","created_by":"Theodore Gast","updated_at":"2026-03-06T07:03:41Z"}

new_value: Fixed by desugaring Enum.Variant { field: val } to Call with named args in the parser's postfix loop. Also fixed grant statement to use parse_expr_no_struct to avoid the struct body being consumed as part of the expression.

old_value: {"id":"tdsl-zv0s","title":"REPL: add quiet/test mode to suppress effect log output","description":"When running .ttrpg-cli test scripts, every RollDice, ActionStarted, MutateField, etc. event prints to stdout, making output very noisy. A --quiet or --test flag for 'ttrpg run' would suppress effect handler log lines and only show errors/assertion failures. This would make .ttrpg-cli scripts practical as a test runner.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"feature","owner":"tfgast@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-03-06T07:03:41Z","created_by":"Theodore Gast","updated_at":"2026-03-06T07:03:41Z"}

new_value: Added --quiet flag to suppress effect log output

old_value: {"id":"tdsl-wsdi","title":"REPL: struct result multi-field assertion requires repeated calls","description":"When a mechanic returns a struct (e.g. resolve_melee_attack returns AttackResult), testing multiple fields requires calling the function multiple times with fresh dice rolls since each eval is independent. Consider supporting let bindings in scripts (e.g. 'let result = call resolve_melee_attack(...)') or multi-field assert syntax to avoid redundant calls.","status":"open","priority":3,"issue_type":"feature","owner":"tfgast@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-03-06T07:03:41Z","created_by":"Theodore Gast","updated_at":"2026-03-06T07:03:41Z"}

new_value: Added let command for binding expression results to variables in REPL/scripts

old_value: {"id":"tdsl-2i49","title":"Bare 'Cleric' variant is ambiguous in spawn blocks","description":"When spawning a Character with class: Cleric inside a spawn block, the REPL reports 'undefined variable Cleric'. Must qualify as Class.Cleric. Other class variants (Fighter, Thief, etc.) resolve fine unqualified. Likely a name collision with something else named Cleric (e.g. a spell list or turn undead context).","status":"open","priority":3,"issue_type":"bug","owner":"tfgast@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-03-06T08:07:44Z","created_by":"Theodore Gast","updated_at":"2026-03-06T08:07:44Z"}

new_value: Fixed by adding type-hinted variant resolution to struct literal evaluation. When a struct field has a known enum type, bare ambiguous variants are now disambiguated via the field type hint.

old_value: {"id":"tdsl-85tc","title":"Support asserting on action return values in CLI scripts","description":"The 'do' command executes actions but discards the return value. Tests that need to verify action results (e.g. ResistSpell returning some(true/false)) cannot assert on the outcome. Proposal: allow 'let result = do ActionName(args)' or similar syntax so scripts can capture and assert on action return values. Currently these tests must stay in Rust.","status":"open","priority":3,"issue_type":"feature","owner":"tfgast@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-03-06T08:07:44Z","created_by":"Theodore Gast","updated_at":"2026-03-06T08:07:44Z"}

new_value: Fixed by switching eval() to use TrackedInterpreter, so action calls work as expressions in let bindings

{"design":"Option A: assert_match \u003cexpr\u003e, EnumName.Variant. Parse with split_top_level_commas, split pattern on dot, compare enum_name and variant fields. Ignores payload fields.","status":"in_progress"}
old_value: {"id":"tdsl-js8k","title":"Add assert_match command for variant-name checking","description":"Add assert_match command that checks an enum variant's name without comparing fields. Rust integration tests constantly do: match \u0026val { Value::EnumVariant { variant, .. } =\u003e assert_eq\\!(variant, \"Turned\") }. CLI equivalent would be: assert_match result, TurnOutcome.Turned — verifies the variant is Turned without caring about field values like number_affected. Unblocks ~15 assertions in turn_undead, morale, and conditions tests. Needs: new Command variant, parser support in commands.rs, and implementation in runner/assert.rs.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"feature","owner":"tfgast@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-03-06T16:47:49Z","created_by":"Theodore Gast","updated_at":"2026-03-06T16:47:49Z"}

new_value: Implemented assert_match command: Command variant, parser, runner dispatch, assertion logic, completer, highlighter, help entry, and unit tests.

{"id":"tdsl-mhta","title":"CLI runner rejects monster MeleeAttack overload in .ttrpg-cli scripts","description":"While porting tdsl-mkps.3, do MeleeAttack(monster, target, MonsterAttack { ... }) in osric/tests/osric_combat.ttrpg-cli failed with [error] too many positional arguments from the CLI runner. The OSRIC DSL defines an overloaded monster MeleeAttack action that requires the extra attack: MonsterAttack argument, so this appears to be a script-runner overload or arity dispatch gap rather than a rules issue. Current workaround in the script is to call resolve_monster_attack(...) and then do TakeDamage(...) manually. Investigate whether .ttrpg-cli action invocation supports overloaded actions with additional parameters on monster receivers.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","owner":"tfgast@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-03-06T21:49:00Z","created_by":"Theodore Gast","updated_at":"2026-03-06T21:49:00Z"}

new_value:
{"design":"## Implementation Plan

**Core insight:** `MeleeAttack` = 'attack with wielded weapon' for any entity. `MonsterMeleeAttack` = 'attack with a natural/innate MonsterAttack entry.'

### Action naming:
- `MeleeAttack on Character (target: entity)` — existing, unchanged
- `MeleeAttack on Monster (target: entity)` — NEW, reads weapon from EquipmentSlots
- `MissileAttack on Character (target: entity, distance: Feet)` — existing, unchanged
- `MissileAttack on Monster (target: entity, distance: Feet)` — NEW, reads weapon from EquipmentSlots
- `MonsterMeleeAttack on Monster (target: entity, attack: MonsterAttack)` — RENAMED from old MeleeAttack
- `MonsterMissileAttack on Monster (target: entity, attack: MonsterAttack, distance: Feet)` — RENAMED from old MissileAttack

### Changes by file:
1. `osric_core.ttrpg` — Add `optional EquipmentSlots` to Monster
2. `osric_equipment.ttrpg` — Widen wielded_melee, wielded_missile, wielded_off_melee, armor_movement_cap to entity with is/has guards
3. `osric_combat.ttrpg` — Rename existing Monster actions to MonsterMeleeAttack/MonsterMissileAttack; add resolve_armed_melee/missile_attack mechanics; add new MeleeAttack/MissileAttack on Monster using wielded weapons
4. Tests — Rename MeleeAttack(monster,...,MonsterAttack) calls to MonsterMeleeAttack; add armed-monster test cases
5. Keep equip/unequip functions Character-only (monsters get equipment at spawn time)

### Design decisions:
- No ability scores on monsters — armed monsters use monster_bthb (hit dice) for to-hit, weapon base damage with no STR modifier
- Compatible with future tdsl-7mn0 (initiative widening to entity) — resolve_action can call combatant.MeleeAttack(target) uniformly","status":"in_progress"}

Intelligent humanoid monsters that wield weapons cannot use these derives, blocking monster weapon combat through the standard pipeline.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","owner":"tfgast@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-03-06T05:49:53Z","created_by":"Theodore Gast","updated_at":"2026-03-06T05:49:53Z"}

new_value: {"notes":"Implementation note: if tdsl-6onz (prompt default body) lands first, the fend-off mechanic can use a prompt with default clause for the defender decision. Otherwise, use direct function calls (Option A) and the integration task (tdsl-1wmm) passes eligible defenders explicitly."}
old_value: {"id":"tdsl-f75j","title":"Implement fend-off mechanic for unarmed combat","description":"Before grappling/overbearing, armed defenders get a free to-hit roll to block the unarmed attack. No damage dealt on success — just stops the attack. Any armed ally adjacent to the defender may also attempt. Exceptions: surprised, invisible attacker, rear/shield-flank. Implement as fend_off_attempt mechanic returning FendOffOutcome. Ref: PG p106.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"tfgast@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-03-07T04:30:54Z","created_by":"Theodore Gast","updated_at":"2026-03-07T04:30:54Z"}

{"id":"tdsl-6onz","title":"Add default body to prompts for auto-resolution","description":"Prompts currently require the host to respond via PromptResult. Add an optional 'default' clause that provides fallback logic when the host returns Response::UseDefault (or a new response variant). This enables prompts to self-resolve in automated contexts (tests, simulations) while still deferring to the host for interactive play. Syntax: prompt foo(...) -\u003e T { hint: '...', suggest: x, default { expr } }. The default body executes in the same scope as suggest, with access to bound parameters. Motivating use case: unarmed combat fend-off decisions where the default is 'all eligible armed defenders attempt to fend off'.","status":"open","priority":3,"issue_type":"feature","owner":"tfgast@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-03-07T05:39:54Z","created_by":"Theodore Gast","updated_at":"2026-03-07T05:39:54Z"}

{"design":"## Decision: Option A — Parameterized Grappling Condition

### Approach
Single parameterized condition with conditional modify logic. No language changes needed.

### Condition Signature
```ttrpg
condition Grappling(
    opponent: Character,
    hold: GrapplingHold,
    is_controlling: bool,
    hold_roll: int
) on bearer: entity { ... }
```

### Verified DSL Capabilities
- Entity-typed condition params: tested (Frightened(source: Character) in parameterized_conditions.rs)
- Enum-typed condition params: works (enums are values everywhere)
- if/else in modify clauses: ModifyStmt::If in AST, checker, and interpreter (phase 1 + phase 2)
- Mixed required + default params on conditions: tested
- ActiveCondition.params stores BTreeMap<Name, Value> at runtime, including Value::Entity

### State Tracking Design
- Each grappled combatant gets their own Grappling condition with their hold type and roll
- Controlling hold determined by comparing hold_roll between the two combatants' conditions
- opponent field links the two sides of the grapple

### Round-to-Round Behavior
- **Maintain hold**: maintain_grappling_hold mechanic auto-inflicts same damage/effects as initial hold
- **New attack**: New GrapplingAttack replaces existing Grappling condition params (remove old, apply new)
- **DecisiveThrow**: Breaks all existing holds, re-establishes new one

### Hold-Specific Effects (via conditional modify)
- All: suppress spellcasting, weapon restriction (dagger-or-smaller)
- RearChoke (controlling): suppress speech/alarm, ally can force-feed substances
- ArmGrab (controlling): may attempt disarm (saving throw vs paralysis)
- WaistLock/ArmLock/HeadLock (controlling): can disengage + prone opponent
- Inferior holds (ElbowBash, LegHold, HeadButt, WickedElbowBash, Gouge): damage only, no special effects

### Multi-Opponent
OSRIC: 'A combatant can only maintain a hold on one opponent.' Each attacker gets Grappling(opponent: target). Pile-on means multiple attackers each have their own condition instance targeting the same defender. The defender only holds one opponent back.

### Out of Scope (separate beads)
- Default conditions on creature types (e.g. FourLegged auto-applied to horses)
- Fend-off action integration (mechanic exists, action does not)"}

Consider parameterized conditions like Grappling(hold: GrapplingHold, position: HoldPosition). {"design":"## `stacking` Clause — Implementation-Ready Design

### Syntax

```
condition <Name>(<params>) on <receiver>: <type>
    stacking <policy>
{
    ...
}
```

### Policies

| Policy | Meaning | Use case |
|--------|---------|----------|
| `all` | Every instance contributes all effects (default, implicit) | Grappling with different opponents |
| `first` | Oldest instance wins; others suppressed | Prone, Stunned — parameterless non-doubling |
| `best by highest(<param>) ties oldest` | Highest param value wins; tie → oldest | Concealed(level), Cover(penalty) |
| `best by lowest(<param>) ties oldest` | Lowest param value wins; tie → oldest | Future: conditions where smaller = stronger |

`ties oldest` is the only tie-breaker for now. Could add `ties newest` later.

### Semantics

For a given bearer, when multiple instances of the same condition exist:

1. Group all active instances by `(condition_name, bearer)`
2. Pick the winner per group according to the stacking policy
3. Suppress all non-winner instances entirely — skip their ancestors and all clause types (modify, suppress, suppress_modify, cost modifiers)

Suppressed instances remain in state. Duration keeps ticking. When the winner expires, next-best becomes the new winner on next evaluation.

### Semantic Details

- **Tie-breaking**: `gained_at` (oldest first). If `gained_at` ties, break by lowest `condition_id` for full determinism.
- **BestBy.param**: Must name an `int` parameter declared on that condition. Inherited parent params do not qualify unless the child also declares them.
- **Inheritance**: The `stacking` clause belongs to the concrete declared condition and is NOT merged across `extends`. Precedence is decided by the child condition's own policy. The winning instance still contributes inherited parent clauses.

### Shared Helper

A single helper computes the allowlist for all conditions on a bearer:

```rust
fn compute_stacking_winners(
    conditions: &[ActiveCondition],
    program: &Program,
) -> HashSet<u64>  // set of winning condition_ids
```

- For `All` policy: all instance ids are included (cheap)
- For `First`: oldest instance wins per (name, bearer) group
- For `BestBy`: highest/lowest param value wins, ties by gained_at then condition_id

The allowlist is computed once per bearer, then passed to all three collection paths.

### Winner Filter Placement

Apply the winner filter BEFORE clause collection, not after. If a condition instance loses precedence, skip its ancestor chain walk and all clause types entirely. Simpler and safer.

### Affected Code Paths

| Location | What it collects | Change |
|----------|-----------------|--------|
| `pipeline.rs` `collect_modifiers_owned` | modify + suppress_modify clauses | Check allowlist before walking ancestors |
| `action.rs` | cost modifiers | Check allowlist before collecting |
| `event.rs` | suppress clauses | Check allowlist before collecting |

### AST Representation

```rust
pub enum StackingPolicy {
    All,
    First,
    BestBy {
        param: Name,
        direction: Direction,
    },
}

pub enum Direction {
    Highest,
    Lowest,
}
```

Tie-breaking is always oldest (lowest gained_at, then lowest condition_id). Extend with TieBreaker enum later if needed.

### Not In Scope

- Cross-condition groups (Concealed vs Invisible) — separate feature
- `best by <expr>` — future extension, single param only for now
- Duplicate-scope (same source vs different source) — separate concept
- Lifecycle uniqueness (reject/replace on apply) — orthogonal to effect precedence"}
old_value: {"id":"tdsl-wnz9","title":"Design: modify clause stacking/exclusivity control","description":"Currently multiple conditions modifying the same derive use strict last-writer-wins (oldest condition first). This works for additive modifiers (Prone +4 + RearAttacked +2 stack because both increment attack_mod). But fails for: concealment not stacking with invisibility (PG p96), exclusive damage resistances, qualitative overrides (AutoHit vs attack_bonus). Possible designs: (A) stack_group attribute on modify clauses for exclusivity, (B) priority levels, (C) additive vs override semantics per field. Lower priority for OSRIC since most modifiers are additive and the few non-stacking cases can be handled with guard conditions in the modify clauses themselves.","status":"open","priority":3,"issue_type":"feature","owner":"tfgast@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-03-04T06:00:28Z","created_by":"Theodore Gast","updated_at":"2026-03-04T06:00:28Z"}
