{"design":"## Design: DSL-Side Entity Construction

### Motivation
Entity creation is currently host-only (CLI spawn command or Rust GameState::add_entity()). To build bestiaries, character factories, or any DSL-side entity creation, we need entity construction as a DSL expression.

### Syntax
Use the same syntax as struct literals — no new keyword. The checker already knows which names are entities vs structs.

```ttrpg
let ogre = Monster {
    name: "Ogre",
    hit_dice: 4d8+1,
    max_hp: 20,
    ac: 5,
    attacks: [MonsterAttack { name: "Club", damage: 1d10 }],
    EquipmentSlots {
        wielded_main: some(WieldedItem.Melee(MeleeWeapon.Club)),
    }
}
```

### Disambiguation
Inside the braces, the parser distinguishes:
- `name: expr` → field init (StructFieldInit)
- `Name { ... }` → group init (GroupInit) — PascalCase ident followed by `{`

### AST Changes
Add `groups: Vec<GroupInit>` to `ExprKind::StructLit` and a new `GroupInit` struct:
```rust
pub struct GroupInit {
    pub name: Name,
    pub fields: Vec<StructFieldInit>,
    pub span: Span,
}
```

### Checker Changes
- Remove the DeclInfo::Entity error in check_struct_lit
- For entities: validate base fields + inline groups against entity schema
- Enforce entity construction only in mutating contexts (function/action/reaction/hook)
- Return Ty::Entity(Name)

### Interpreter Changes
- When StructLit name resolves to entity type:
  - Evaluate fields + fill defaults
  - Call state.add_entity() to register
  - Process inline groups (evaluate + write as fields)
  - Auto-materialize required include groups with defaults
  - Initialize resource fields
  - Return Value::Entity(entity_ref)

### Context Restriction
Entity construction mutates GameState, so only allowed in: function, action, reaction, hook. Not in: derive, mechanic, table, const. The checker already tracks mutability context.

### Key Files
- AST: crates/ttrpg_ast/src/ast.rs (StructLit variant, new GroupInit)
- Parser: crates/ttrpg_parser/src/expr.rs (parse_struct_lit, parse_struct_field_init)
- Checker: crates/ttrpg_checker/src/check_literal.rs (check_struct_lit)
- Interpreter: crates/ttrpg_interp/src/eval/dispatch.rs (StructLit eval)
- CLI spawn reference: crates/ttrpg_cli/src/runner/mutation.rs (existing spawn logic to mirror)"}
old_value: {"id":"tdsl-97st","title":"DSL-side entity construction via struct literal syntax","description":"Enable creating entities directly in DSL code using the same syntax as struct literals (e.g., Monster { name: \"Ogre\", ... }), removing the need for host-only spawn. Includes parser support for inline optional groups (GroupName { ... } inside the literal), checker validation, interpreter entity creation, and context restriction to mutating blocks only.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"feature","owner":"tfgast@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-03-08T02:20:40Z","created_by":"Theodore Gast","updated_at":"2026-03-08T02:20:40Z"}

Extend ExprKind::StructLit to include a groups: Vec\u003cGroupInit\u003e field alongside the existing fields and base. Update VisitSpansMut and arbitrary impls to handle the new field. This is a data-only change — parser, checker, and interpreter will initially pass empty vecs.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","owner":"tfgast@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-03-08T02:46:23Z","created_by":"Theodore Gast","updated_at":"2026-03-08T02:46:23Z"}

Collect them into the new groups vec on StructLit.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","owner":"tfgast@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-03-08T02:46:26Z","created_by":"Theodore Gast","updated_at":"2026-03-08T02:46:26Z"}

{"id":"tdsl-bjq4","title":"HP roll mechanic for character creation","description":"Roll starting HP at level 1. Use class hit die from class_def. Apply CON modifier (con_hp_mod for non-fighters, con_hp_mod_fighter for Fighter/Paladin/Ranger). Minimum 1 HP. For Ranger, add bonus_first_hd (+1 at 1st level). For multi-class, roll each class HD, add CON mod, divide by number of classes (drop fractions), min 1. Record HpRoll entries.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"tfgast@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-03-08T04:04:21Z","created_by":"Theodore Gast","updated_at":"2026-03-08T04:04:21Z"}

{"id":"tdsl-rsr8","title":"Exceptional strength roll for fighter-types","description":"If character is a Fighter, Paladin, or Ranger with STR 18, roll d100 for exceptional strength percentile. Non-fighter-types with STR 18 just have plain 18.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"tfgast@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-03-08T04:04:25Z","created_by":"Theodore Gast","updated_at":"2026-03-08T04:04:25Z"}

{"id":"tdsl-up5f","title":"Starting gold roll mechanic per class","description":"Roll starting gold based on class: Assassin=2d6x10, Cleric=3d6x10, Druid=3d6x10, Fighter=5d4x10, Illusionist=2d4x10, MagicUser=2d4x10, Monk=5d4, Paladin=5d4x10, Ranger=5d4x10, Thief=2d6x10. Per OSRIC 3.0 p.73 Table 1.4.2.1.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"tfgast@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-03-08T04:04:25Z","created_by":"Theodore Gast","updated_at":"2026-03-08T04:04:25Z"}

{"id":"tdsl-xacb","title":"Optional group activation during chargen","description":"Based on chosen class(es), activate appropriate optional groups on the Character entity: Spellcasting (if class has spell_progression != NonCaster), ThiefSkills (if class has_thief_skills — compute base skills + ancestry adjustments per Table 1.3.10.4d), TurnUndead (if class can_turn_undead), WeaponSpecialization (if class weapon_specialization — prompt for specialised weapon, default=none, suggest=none), HenchmanMorale (compute from CHA).","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"tfgast@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-03-08T04:04:25Z","created_by":"Theodore Gast","updated_at":"2026-03-08T04:04:25Z"}

{"id":"tdsl-upzt","title":"Character entity construction and saving throws","description":"Construct the final Character entity from all gathered data: name (prompt, default=random, suggest='Adventurer'), classes list (ClassLevel with level=1, xp=0), ancestry, alignment, abilities map, HitPoints (max_hp and hp from roll), base_movement from ancestry, gold from roll, saving_throws from class saving throw table at level 1. Return the fully constructed Character.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"tfgast@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-03-08T04:04:25Z","created_by":"Theodore Gast","updated_at":"2026-03-08T04:04:25Z"}

old_value: {"id":"tdsl-cztn","title":"Align restricted-class weapon lists with OSRIC tables","description":"Restricted-class weapon permissions are missing some weapons explicitly allowed by OSRIC. In the current equipment/class restriction model, druids lose hammer access and illusionists lose both dart and staff access even though those weapons are listed as allowed. Reference: OSRIC PG §1.3.3.1 and §1.3.5.1. Affected file: osric/osric_equipment.ttrpg.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","owner":"tfgast@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-03-09T07:03:09Z","created_by":"Theodore Gast","updated_at":"2026-03-09T07:03:09Z"}

old_value: {"id":"tdsl-8jrd","title":"Honor memorization and interruption in initiative spellcasting","description":"Initiative spell resolution bypasses the main CastSpell action. It begins casting and later resolves the spell directly, which skips memorized-slot consumption and other CastSpell gating, and it can still complete a spell after the caster has taken damage because the queued action is resolved even after the casting condition is removed. OSRIC requires declared spells to be memorized and lost when interrupted before taking effect. Reference: OSRIC PG §1.8.1 and §1.6.2.11. Affected files: osric/osric_initiative.ttrpg, osric/osric_spells.ttrpg.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","owner":"tfgast@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-03-09T07:01:50Z","created_by":"Theodore Gast","updated_at":"2026-03-09T07:01:50Z"}

{"id":"tdsl-scn6","title":"Preserve the rescue window after failed lethal poison saves","description":"apply_poison() currently treats a failed lethal poison save as immediate death. OSRIC states that poison death is not instantaneous and explicitly leaves a window for Slow Poison or Neutralise Poison to save the victim. Reference: OSRIC PG §1.6.9.1. Affected file: osric/osric_afflictions.ttrpg.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","owner":"tfgast@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-03-09T07:02:22Z","created_by":"Theodore Gast","updated_at":"2026-03-09T07:02:22Z"}

{"id":"tdsl-jtcy","title":"Make monster immunities, resistances, and vulnerabilities affect damage","description":"Monster traits such as ImmuneToFire, ImmuneToCold, ImmuneToAcid, ImmuneToLightning, VulnerableToFire, and ResistantToBlunt are currently empty conditions with no consuming damage hooks. As a result, many bestiary entries do not actually change damage resolution the way OSRIC specifies. Reference: OSRIC GM Guide monster entries such as skeletons, trolls, dragons, gelatinous cubes, and efreet. Affected files: osric/osric_monster_traits.ttrpg, osric/osric_bestiary.ttrpg, and combat damage resolution as needed.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","owner":"tfgast@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-03-09T07:02:51Z","created_by":"Theodore Gast","updated_at":"2026-03-09T07:02:51Z"}

new_value: Implemented monster damage-type hooks, corrected skeleton resistance wiring, and passed targeted OSRIC combat/bestiary/spell tests

old_value: {"id":"tdsl-mp3w","title":"Use the thief-bearing class for multiclass thief skill checks","description":"Legal multiclass builds such as fighter/thief should use the thief-class table for thief abilities. In osric/osric_thief_skills.ttrpg, character_thief_skill() reads first(char.classes), so a fighter/thief or cleric/thief can get 0% skills whenever thief is not the first class entry. Reference: OSRIC PG §1.3.11 and ancestry multiclass notes such as §1.2.2.3 and §1.2.4.3. Affected file: osric/osric_thief_skills.ttrpg.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","owner":"tfgast@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-03-09T07:01:07Z","created_by":"Theodore Gast","updated_at":"2026-03-09T07:01:07Z"}

{"id":"tdsl-uhri","title":"Use least-restrictive multiclass armour permissions","description":"OSRIC PG ancestry multiclass rules say elves and half-elves use the least restrictive armour restrictions across their classes (for example, fighter/magic-user may wear plate; magic-user/thief may wear studded leather). In osric/osric_multiclass.ttrpg, effective_armour_permission() ranks permissions from NoArmour to AnyArmour and then takes the minimum rank, which enforces the most restrictive rule instead. This blocks legal multiclass equipment usage and contradicts the quoted examples. Reference: PG §1.3.11 and ancestry multiclass notes in §1.2.2.3 and §1.2.4.3. Affected file: osric/osric_multiclass.ttrpg.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","owner":"tfgast@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-03-09T06:58:18Z","created_by":"Theodore Gast","updated_at":"2026-03-09T06:58:18Z"}

{"id":"tdsl-jrqx","title":"Correct turn undead numeric-result handling for evil clerics and fiend targets","description":"On numbered turn-undead table results, the implementation always returns Turned(2d6). OSRIC has additional branches here: fiends and paladins are affected in 1d2 numbers, evil clerics can control rather than merely turn on destroy/control results, and paladin-specific exceptions differ from fiend handling. The current collapse loses those distinctions. Reference: OSRIC PG §1.6.5 and Table 1.6.5a. Affected file: osric/osric_turn_undead.ttrpg.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","owner":"tfgast@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-03-09T07:02:22Z","created_by":"Theodore Gast","updated_at":"2026-03-09T07:02:22Z"}

{"id":"tdsl-671j","title":"Complete potion effects and fix Potion of Invulnerability","description":"DrinkPotion only resolves a subset of potion types and falls through to an event-only default for many named potions, so numerous GM Guide potions currently have no game effect. Potion of Invulnerability is also implemented incorrectly: OSRIC gives 3d6+2 rounds plus broader protections and saving-throw bonuses, while the current code uses 5d4+2 rounds and only a +2 AC effect. Reference: OSRIC GM Guide §2.13.2. Affected file: osric/osric_potions.ttrpg.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","owner":"tfgast@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-03-09T07:02:51Z","created_by":"Theodore Gast","updated_at":"2026-03-09T07:02:51Z"}

{"id":"tdsl-infj","title":"Correct undead weapon-damage exceptions in the bestiary","description":"Several undead weapon-damage exceptions are encoded incorrectly. Skeletons are currently resistant to blunt instead of to slashing and piercing, and wights/wraiths use a generic ImmuneToNormalWeapons trait that incorrectly blocks non-magical silver weapons even though OSRIC says silver can still harm them. Reference: OSRIC GM Guide monster entries for skeletons, wights, and wraiths. Affected files: osric/osric_bestiary.ttrpg, osric/osric_monster_traits.ttrpg.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","owner":"tfgast@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-03-09T07:02:51Z","created_by":"Theodore Gast","updated_at":"2026-03-09T07:02:51Z"}

{"id":"tdsl-infj","title":"Correct undead weapon-damage exceptions in the bestiary","description":"Several undead weapon-damage exceptions are encoded incorrectly. Skeletons are currently resistant to blunt instead of to slashing and piercing, and wights/wraiths use a generic ImmuneToNormalWeapons trait that incorrectly blocks non-magical silver weapons even though OSRIC says silver can still harm them. Reference: OSRIC GM Guide monster entries for skeletons, wights, and wraiths. Affected files: osric/osric_bestiary.ttrpg, osric/osric_monster_traits.ttrpg.","status":"in_progress","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","owner":"tfgast@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-03-09T07:02:51Z","created_by":"Theodore Gast","updated_at":"2026-03-09T16:18:32Z"}

{"id":"tdsl-1emm","title":"Correct spell-scroll class eligibility","description":"Spell scroll eligibility is wrong in two directions. The current logic allows paladins to use divine spell scrolls and rangers to use arcane spell scrolls, which OSRIC does not allow, and it also omits the 10th-level thief Read Scrolls exception for arcane/phantasmal/druidic scrolls. Reference: OSRIC GM Guide §2.13.3.1 and PG §1.3.10.3. Affected file: osric/osric_scrolls.ttrpg.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","owner":"tfgast@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-03-09T07:01:50Z","created_by":"Theodore Gast","updated_at":"2026-03-09T07:01:50Z"}

{"id":"tdsl-8r0z","title":"Delay paladin Turn Undead until 3rd level","description":"OSRIC gives paladins Turn Undead at 3rd level, not at 1st level. The current class definition marks paladins as turners unconditionally, and chargen grants the TurnUndead group immediately for any class list containing paladin. Reference: OSRIC PG §1.3.8.2 ('3rd Level—Turn Undead'). Affected files: osric/osric_class.ttrpg, osric/osric_chargen.ttrpg.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","owner":"tfgast@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-03-09T07:01:07Z","created_by":"Theodore Gast","updated_at":"2026-03-09T07:01:07Z"}

{"id":"tdsl-6xpm","title":"Fix ranger hit-die progression in chargen and level-up","description":"OSRIC rangers receive 2d8 hit points at 1st level and a total of 11 hit dice before switching to fixed +2 hp per level. The DSL currently models rangers as 1d8 + 1 at level 1 via bonus_first_hd, and class_def sets max_hit_dice to 10, so ranger HP is understated both at character creation and across later advancement. Reference: OSRIC PG §1.3.9.1 and Table 1.3.9.4a. Affected files: osric/osric_class.ttrpg, osric/osric_chargen.ttrpg, osric/osric_levelup.ttrpg.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","owner":"tfgast@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-03-09T07:01:07Z","created_by":"Theodore Gast","updated_at":"2026-03-09T07:01:07Z"}

{"id":"tdsl-o8ir","title":"Use class-specific minimum levels for spell-scroll failure chances","description":"The current scroll failure logic uses a generic spell_level*2-1 heuristic instead of the actual class-specific minimum level needed to cast the spell from memory. OSRIC bases under-level scroll failure on that real minimum class level, and the GM Guide example for a 2nd-level cleric using a 2nd-level cleric spell shows 10% failure, not the 5% this heuristic would produce. Reference: OSRIC GM Guide §2.13.3.1. Affected file: osric/osric_scrolls.ttrpg.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","owner":"tfgast@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-03-09T07:01:50Z","created_by":"Theodore Gast","updated_at":"2026-03-09T07:01:50Z"}

{"id":"tdsl-qqgt","title":"Persist rolled starting gold into character inventory","description":"osric/osric_chargen.ttrpg rolls starting gold into a local gold variable, but that value is never written to Character or Inventory state. Newly created characters therefore start with zero coins despite OSRIC Table 1.4.2.1. Reference: PG Chapter 4 equipment and starting gold table. Affected files: osric/osric_chargen.ttrpg, and inventory initialization if coin denomination handling is needed.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","owner":"tfgast@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-03-09T07:00:31Z","created_by":"Theodore Gast","updated_at":"2026-03-09T07:00:31Z"}

{"id":"tdsl-mlq3","title":"Split normal sleep from magical sleep in combat conditions","description":"The single Sleeping condition treats all sleep as helpless automatic-hit, maximum-damage sleep. OSRIC distinguishes normal sleep from magical sleep: ordinary sleepers are attacked at +4, lose shield/DEX benefits, and use a separate assassination-style kill chance instead of full magical-helplessness handling. Reference: OSRIC PG §1.6.10.12-§1.6.10.13. Affected file: osric/osric_conditions.ttrpg.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","owner":"tfgast@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-03-09T07:02:22Z","created_by":"Theodore Gast","updated_at":"2026-03-09T07:02:22Z"}

{"id":"tdsl-g686","title":"Implement scroll of warding protections","description":"The warding-scroll conditions are currently inert markers with no rule-consuming effects, so reading a scroll of warding does not actually confer the protection OSRIC specifies. Reference: OSRIC GM Guide §2.13.3.2. Affected file: osric/osric_scrolls.ttrpg.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","owner":"tfgast@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-03-09T07:02:51Z","created_by":"Theodore Gast","updated_at":"2026-03-09T07:02:51Z"}

{"id":"tdsl-xqb8","title":"Fix fighter-group extra-attack level thresholds and timing","description":"Fighter-group melee attack combinations start too early and use the wrong segment timing. The current rules start the 3/2 progression at level 7 and place the single-attack round on segment 1, but OSRIC gives fighters/paladins/rangers the extra attack starting at 8th level and says the non-extra-attack round uses normal initiative timing rather than an automatic segment-1 strike. Reference: OSRIC PG §1.3.4.3, §1.3.8.3, §1.3.9.3, and §1.6.1.3. Affected files: osric/osric_initiative.ttrpg, osric/osric_combat.ttrpg.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","owner":"tfgast@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-03-09T07:02:22Z","created_by":"Theodore Gast","updated_at":"2026-03-09T07:02:22Z"}

{"id":"tdsl-bv84","title":"Store per-class multiclass HP rolls for drain/restoration","description":"At level 1, osric/osric_chargen.ttrpg computes one averaged multiclass HP total and writes that same amount into every HpRoll entry for every class. osric/osric_level_drain.ttrpg later looks up HP by (class, level) and subtracts the recorded amount when one class is drained. That means draining one class can remove the character's full averaged level-1 HP instead of just that class's share. The hp_rolls history needs to record per-class averaged gains, not duplicate the total across classes. Reference: PG §1.3.11 multi-class hit points and §1.6.7 level drain. Affected files: osric/osric_chargen.ttrpg, osric/osric_level_drain.ttrpg.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","owner":"tfgast@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-03-09T06:58:18Z","created_by":"Theodore Gast","updated_at":"2026-03-09T06:58:18Z"}

{"id":"tdsl-n0vk","title":"Require surprise and valid anatomy for assassination attempts","description":"OSRIC assassination is only available when the assassin surprises the target, and many creatures are ineligible because they lack vital organs (for example slimes, oozes, elementals, golems, and zombies). The current Assassinate action only checks for assassin class and a melee weapon, so it can be used in ordinary melee and against ineligible targets. Reference: OSRIC PG §1.3.1.2. Affected file: osric/osric_combat.ttrpg.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","owner":"tfgast@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-03-09T07:01:07Z","created_by":"Theodore Gast","updated_at":"2026-03-09T07:01:07Z"}

{"id":"tdsl-8vk4","title":"Set-against-charge weapons always strike before the charger","description":"OSRIC states that a weapon properly set against a charge attacks before the charging opponent, and on a hit it deals double damage. The current initiative/combat resolution still compares weapon lengths even after SetAgainstChargeAction is declared, so a charger with an equal or longer weapon can incorrectly strike first. Reference: OSRIC PG §1.6.2.1 and §1.6.2.10. Affected files: osric/osric_initiative.ttrpg, osric/osric_combat.ttrpg.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","owner":"tfgast@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-03-09T07:01:07Z","created_by":"Theodore Gast","updated_at":"2026-03-09T07:01:07Z"}

new_value: Implemented in scroll system; follow-on created for missing creature taxonomy markers

old_value: {"id":"tdsl-afp4","title":"Use OSRIC XP rates for kept magic items","description":"Inventory treasure XP currently treats kept magic items as if they granted full gp-value treasure XP. OSRIC instead awards 1/10 of base value for kept magic items and only 1/20 for kept potions, with full value applying when items are sold. Reference: OSRIC PG §1.5.6 and GM Guide §2.1.4. Affected files: osric/osric_inventory.ttrpg, osric/osric_xp.ttrpg, osric/osric_potions.ttrpg, osric/osric_scrolls.ttrpg.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","owner":"tfgast@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-03-09T07:01:50Z","created_by":"Theodore Gast","updated_at":"2026-03-09T07:01:50Z"}

{"id":"tdsl-ihe2","title":"Model arcane spellbooks and understood spells","description":"Arcane memorization currently accepts any implemented arcane spell because the character model has no spellbook contents, known-spell state, or 'understood spell' tracking. OSRIC requires magic-users and illusionists to copy spells into spellbooks and successfully understand them before memorization. Reference: OSRIC PG §1.3.5.2, §1.3.6.2, and spell acquisition tables §1.3.5.2a / §1.3.6.2a. Affected files: osric/osric_core.ttrpg, osric/osric_spells.ttrpg, osric/osric_chargen.ttrpg, osric/osric_levelup.ttrpg.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"feature","owner":"tfgast@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-03-09T07:01:50Z","created_by":"Theodore Gast","updated_at":"2026-03-09T07:01:50Z"}

{"id":"tdsl-fjnt","title":"Support multi-tradition spell slots for rangers and multi-class casters","description":"The current Spellcasting model stores one spell_slots list and one memorised_spells list. Chargen picks only the first caster class in a multiclass build, and level-up overwrites Spellcasting.spell_slots using the class that just advanced. That cannot represent legal OSRIC cases such as cleric/magic-user and cleric/fighter/magic-user, and it also drops the ranger's separate magic-user spell progression even though ranger_mu_slots() exists. Reference: PG §1.3.9 ranger spellcasting, §1.3.11 multiclassing, and class spell tables in Chapter 3. Affected files: osric/osric_spells.ttrpg, osric/osric_chargen.ttrpg, osric/osric_levelup.ttrpg, osric/osric_core.ttrpg.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","owner":"tfgast@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-03-09T06:58:18Z","created_by":"Theodore Gast","updated_at":"2026-03-09T06:58:18Z"}

{"id":"tdsl-mxez","title":"Validate memorized slot level against the spell's true level","description":"MemoriseSpell and CastSpell trust the caller-supplied spell_level and never verify it against the actual level of the chosen SpellId. That allows implemented spells to be placed into or cast from the wrong slot level, which violates OSRIC's same-level memorization requirement. Reference: OSRIC PG §1.8.1 and class spellcasting rules in Chapter 3. Affected files: osric/osric_spells.ttrpg, osric/osric_cleric_spells.ttrpg, osric/osric_mu_spells.ttrpg, osric/osric_spell_registry.ttrpg.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","owner":"tfgast@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-03-09T07:01:50Z","created_by":"Theodore Gast","updated_at":"2026-03-09T07:01:50Z"}

{"id":"tdsl-6imo","title":"Add creature taxonomy markers consumed by scroll wardings","description":"While resolving tdsl-g686, the scroll system gained active protections for demon/devil/elemental/lycanthrope wardings, but the wider OSRIC model still does not tag creatures with the condition markers those protections inspect (for example Demon, Devil, Elemental, Werewolf, and related subtype markers). Add those taxonomy markers in the bestiary/monster traits so the new warding protections apply end-to-end against real creatures.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","owner":"tfgast@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-03-09T16:43:50Z","created_by":"Theodore Gast","updated_at":"2026-03-09T16:43:50Z"}

{"id":"tdsl-smak","title":"Correct magical item-save bonuses and artifact save targets","description":"Item saving throw bonuses and artifact/relic save targets do not match OSRIC Table 1.6.4a. The code uses 2 + magic_bonus, so a +2 item effectively saves at +4 instead of +3, and artifacts/relics are hardcoded to save on 2 even though the guide gives 2 or 3 depending on item. Reference: OSRIC PG §1.6.4 and Table 1.6.4a. Affected file: osric/osric_item_saves.ttrpg.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","owner":"tfgast@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-03-09T07:02:22Z","created_by":"Theodore Gast","updated_at":"2026-03-09T07:02:22Z"}

{"id":"tdsl-xrkr","title":"Fix silver-piece to copper-piece conversions in standard gear","description":"Several standard gear entries that are priced in silver pieces are stored as raw silver values instead of copper-piece values, for example bedroll, hammer, oil, and wooden holy symbol. That makes common equipment ten times cheaper than OSRIC lists. Reference: OSRIC PG Table 1.4.2.3A. Affected file: osric/osric_inventory.ttrpg.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","owner":"tfgast@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-03-09T07:02:51Z","created_by":"Theodore Gast","updated_at":"2026-03-09T07:02:51Z"}

{"id":"tdsl-zbr0","title":"Make prone creatures unable to attack","description":"OSRIC says a prone character or monster cannot attack. The current Prone condition removes shield and DEX AC bonuses and grants attackers +4, but it does not suppress the prone creature's own attacks. Reference: OSRIC PG §1.6.10.11. Affected file: osric/osric_conditions.ttrpg.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","owner":"tfgast@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-03-09T07:02:22Z","created_by":"Theodore Gast","updated_at":"2026-03-09T07:02:22Z"}

{"id":"tdsl-3jvh","title":"Complete invisibility combat effects and prevent concealment stacking","description":"The Invisible condition currently applies only the -4 to-hit penalty against attackers. OSRIC also gives invisible creatures +4 on saving throws, and concealment explicitly does not stack with invisibility. The current condition model misses the save bonus and allows Concealed to stack on top of Invisible. Reference: OSRIC PG §1.6.3.1, §1.6.3.6, and §1.6.10.8. Affected file: osric/osric_conditions.ttrpg.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","owner":"tfgast@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-03-09T07:02:22Z","created_by":"Theodore Gast","updated_at":"2026-03-09T07:02:22Z"}

{"id":"tdsl-z5ny","title":"Apply Bless and Curse morale modifiers to morale checks","description":"Bless and Curse currently modify attack rolls only. OSRIC also gives Bless a +5% morale benefit and Curse a -5% morale penalty, but the morale system does not consult either condition. Reference: OSRIC PG §1.8.2.1. Affected files: osric/osric_cleric_spells.ttrpg, osric/osric_morale.ttrpg.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","owner":"tfgast@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-03-09T07:03:09Z","created_by":"Theodore Gast","updated_at":"2026-03-09T07:03:09Z"}

{"id":"tdsl-4fs7","title":"Enforce Hold Person's 1-3 target limit","description":"Cleric Hold Person is limited to 1-3 targets, with save modifiers based on choosing one, two, or three creatures. The current resolver accepts arbitrary target-list length and treats any count above two as the three-target case instead of rejecting four or more targets. Reference: OSRIC PG §1.8.2.2. Affected file: osric/osric_cleric_spells.ttrpg.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","owner":"tfgast@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-03-09T07:03:09Z","created_by":"Theodore Gast","updated_at":"2026-03-09T07:03:09Z"}

{"id":"tdsl-c22j","title":"Honor assassin and illusionist prime requisites in dual-class checks","description":"OSRIC dual-classing requires 15+ in the old class prime requisite(s) and 17+ in the new class prime requisite(s). osric/osric_dualclass.ttrpg treats Assassin and Illusionist as having no prime requisites, so dual-class eligibility becomes vacuously true for those classes. The Player Guide explicitly identifies Dexterity as the prime requisite for assassins and Intelligence as the prime requisite for illusionists. Reference: PG §1.3.12, §1.1.3, and §1.1.5. Affected file: osric/osric_dualclass.ttrpg.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","owner":"tfgast@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-03-09T06:58:18Z","created_by":"Theodore Gast","updated_at":"2026-03-09T06:58:18Z"}

{"id":"tdsl-58a6","title":"Use paladin and ranger special casting levels in spell scaling","description":"Shared caster_level() ignores paladin and ranger special casting-level derives, so any scaling spell effect used by those classes reads caster level 0 instead of their OSRIC special casting level. Reference: OSRIC PG paladin and ranger spellcasting rules in §1.3.8.3 and §1.3.9.3. Affected files: osric/osric_spells.ttrpg, osric/osric_class.ttrpg.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","owner":"tfgast@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-03-09T07:03:09Z","created_by":"Theodore Gast","updated_at":"2026-03-09T07:03:09Z"}

{"id":"tdsl-l56k","title":"Wire dual-class progression rules into live character state","description":"The dual-class module defines eligibility and rule helpers, but the active character lifecycle does not create ClassingMode.Dual characters or enforce dual-class advancement restrictions. As written, the old-class ability lockout, HP suppression until the new class exceeds the old, and related progression rules are not connected to real state transitions. Reference: OSRIC PG §1.3.12. Affected files: osric/osric_dualclass.ttrpg, osric/osric_levelup.ttrpg, osric/osric_core.ttrpg, and any chargen/class transition entry point.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","owner":"tfgast@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-03-09T07:01:07Z","created_by":"Theodore Gast","updated_at":"2026-03-09T07:01:07Z"}

{"id":"tdsl-e2o3","title":"Model silver weapon materials for undead immunity exceptions","description":"While addressing tdsl-infj, the bestiary/trait side could distinguish wights and wraiths from creatures that truly require magical weapons, but combat still has no weapon material signal to consume that distinction. Attack resolution only sees weapon_bonus / magic_bonus, so non-magical silver weapons cannot currently bypass the new silver-exception trait. Add weapon material or a silver marker to wielded weapons and thread it through combat resolution so immunity traits can admit silver hits where OSRIC allows them.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","owner":"tfgast@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-03-09T16:25:02Z","created_by":"Theodore Gast","updated_at":"2026-03-09T16:25:02Z"}

In osric_initiative.ttrpg, movement_per_segment and movement_through_segment had to use .value field access and manual reconstruction: Feet { value: floor(base_movement.value / 10) } instead of the natural: floor(base_movement / 10). The spec covers unit/unit division (producing float) and int*unit scaling, but unit/int (producing same unit) is missing. new_value: {"description":"Added div operator for floor division: int div int -\u003e int, unit div int -\u003e unit (scale down), unit div unit -\u003e int (floor ratio). Implemented as a reserved keyword at same precedence as * / %.","title":"Floor division operator (div) for int and unit types"}

{"description":"Make tags from condition declarations (e.g. #magical, #curse) accessible at runtime on ActiveCondition values. Expose as c.tags field (Set<String>) so DSL code can categorically filter conditions by their declaration tags. Orthogonal to EffectSource — tags are properties of the condition type, source is per-application data.

Condition-level tags describe identity (what the condition IS: #magical, #curse, #divine) vs modify-clause tags which describe individual effects (what the effect DOES: #bonus, #penalty). Condition tags enable queries like 'remove all #curse conditions' without needing to inspect modify clause internals.

Steps:
1. Add tags: Vec<Name> to ConditionDecl in AST
2. Parse #tag annotations on condition declarations
3. Carry tags through checker (validate against declared tag namespace)
4. Store tags on ActiveCondition at runtime
5. Expose as Set<String> via ActiveCondition.to_value()
6. Document condition-level tags in spec, language reference, and authoring guide — clarify distinction from modify-clause tags"}

new_value:
{"description":"Make tags from condition declarations (e.g. #curse, #disease, #poison) accessible at runtime on ActiveCondition values. Expose as c.tags field (Set<String>) so DSL code can categorically filter conditions by their declaration tags.

IMPORTANT DESIGN DISTINCTION:
- Condition-level tags are for INHERENTLY STATIC properties of the condition type — things that are always true regardless of how the condition was applied (e.g. #curse, #disease, #poison). Bestow Curse is always a curse; Mummy Rot is always a disease.
- Source-dependent properties like 'magical' belong on EffectSource, NOT condition tags, because the same condition (e.g. Paralyzed) can come from magical sources (Hold Person spell) or non-magical sources (ghoul venom). Magicalness is a property of the application, not the condition type.
- Modify-clause tags describe individual mechanical effects (what the effect DOES: #bonus, #penalty) and are used for targeted suppression within a condition.

Steps:
1. Add tags: Vec<Name> to ConditionDecl in AST
2. Parse #tag annotations on condition declarations
3. Carry tags through checker (validate against declared tag namespace)
4. Store tags on ActiveCondition at runtime
5. Expose as Set<String> via ActiveCondition.to_value()
6. Document condition-level tags in spec, language reference, and authoring guide — clarify distinction from modify-clause tags and from EffectSource"}

{"description":"Introduce a source-resolution layer ahead of `parse_multi` so callers can load a single entry file and have transitive source dependencies resolved automatically.

Scope:
- Add an `import "path.ttrpg"` directive for file-level source loading, distinct from `use "System"` which remains purely symbol visibility. The source resolver follows `import` edges to build the file graph transitively from an entrypoint.
- `import` appears at file top-level (alongside `use`) and takes a relative path to another .ttrpg file.
- `use "System"` semantics are unchanged: symbol visibility / namespace imports only.
- Canonicalize paths, dedupe files, detect cycles, and produce a stable load order.
- Expose the resolver through the CLI `load` path and any shared loader APIs.

Why:
- Today `crates/ttrpg_cli/src/runner/load.rs` only tokenizes explicit paths and expands globs.
- `crates/ttrpg_parser/src/resolve.rs` validates imports only after the full source set is already loaded.
- Users should be able to load an entrypoint rather than hand-maintain a file list.
- Separating `import` (file loading) from `use` (symbol visibility) makes both concepts explicit and avoids overloading `use` with resolution duties."}

{"id":"tdsl-6ddr.1","title":"Add source resolver for entrypoint-based loading","description":"Introduce a source-resolution layer ahead of `parse_multi` so callers can load a single entry file and have transitive source dependencies resolved automatically.

Scope:
- Add a first-class source-loading concept distinct from module visibility.
- Support relative imports from one source file to another instead of requiring the CLI to enumerate every file or rely on globs.
- Canonicalize paths, dedupe files, detect cycles, and produce a stable load order.
- Preserve current `use "System"` semantics as symbol-visibility/import metadata unless intentionally changed.
- Expose the resolver through the CLI `load` path and any shared loader APIs.

Why:
- Today `crates/ttrpg_cli/src/runner/load.rs` only tokenizes explicit paths and expands globs.
- `crates/ttrpg_parser/src/resolve.rs` validates imports only after the full source set is already loaded.
- Users should be able to load an entrypoint rather than hand-maintain a file list.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"feature","owner":"tfgast@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-03-09T21:50:42Z","created_by":"Theodore Gast","updated_at":"2026-03-09T21:50:42Z"}

new_value:
{"description":"Add a package-level manifest (`ttrpg.toml`) for rule content so callers can load a package or named target instead of relying on raw globs or handwritten file lists.

## Manifest format (v1)

```toml
manifest_version = 1

[package]
name = "ose"
default_target = "full"

[entries.core]
path = "ose_core.ttrpg"

[entries.combat]
path = "ose_combat.ttrpg"

[bundles.full]
entries = ["core", "class", "combat", "equipment", "magic", "spells"]

[bundles.chargen]
entries = ["core", "class", "chargen", "equipment"]
```

## Terminology

- **entry**: A single root source file. The source resolver follows its `import` edges transitively to build the file graph.
- **bundle**: A curated composition of multiple entries. Replaces handwritten source vectors and most globs.
- **target**: Either an entry or a bundle. The unit of CLI loading.
- **default_target**: What `ttrpg load <package>` resolves to (usually a bundle representing the full usable ruleset).

## CLI semantics

- `ttrpg load ose` → load package default target (usually `full` bundle)
- `ttrpg load ose:core` → load single entry
- `ttrpg load ose:chargen` → load named bundle

## Design principles

- The manifest describes package identity, entrypoints, and named bundles only.
- The file graph lives in source files via `import` directives, not in the manifest.
- The manifest should not list every transitive file; the resolver follows imports from entry files.
- `default_target` should be the most useful complete experience, not the minimal seed.

## Deferred to later versions

- Inter-package `[dependencies]` (local path or otherwise)
- Explicit system-name-to-file maps
- Per-file metadata blocks
- Glob patterns inside the manifest
- Publish/registry semantics
- Version constraints"}

{"id":"tdsl-6ddr.3","title":"Add package manifest and named bundle entrypoints for rule modules","description":"Add a package-level manifest for rule content so callers can load a package or named entrypoint instead of relying on raw globs or handwritten file lists.

Scope:
- Define a manifest format for package metadata, root paths, entry files, and named bundles/targets.
- Allow CLI and library callers to load by package + entrypoint, not just by filesystem path list.
- Make load order explicit where needed for bundled rule sets such as OSE/OSRIC.
- Treat glob-based loading as a compatibility path rather than the primary ergonomic workflow.

Why:
- Bundled game data and examples already behave like packages, but the project has no single source of truth for their source inventory.
- A manifest gives stable, reviewable declarations for entrypoints and bundle composition.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"feature","owner":"tfgast@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-03-09T21:51:04Z","created_by":"Theodore Gast","updated_at":"2026-03-09T21:51:04Z"}

{"id":"tdsl-6ddr.2","title":"Record source origins in module metadata and improve missing-module diagnostics","description":"Extend module-loading metadata so systems and imports retain enough source-origin information to explain where a system came from and why it could not be loaded.

Scope:
- Record which file(s) define each system and which source edge introduced them.
- Distinguish source-loading relationships from semantic `use` visibility where needed.
- Improve diagnostics for missing systems/files to report the import chain or source path that led to the failure.
- Surface clearer errors for duplicate system definitions, shadowing, and no-effect imports in multi-file loads.

Why:
- `ModuleMap` and `SystemInfo` currently know names and semantic imports, but not source provenance.
- Once source loading is more automatic, diagnostics need to explain loader decisions instead of only saying a system is unknown.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"tfgast@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-03-09T21:50:51Z","created_by":"Theodore Gast","updated_at":"2026-03-09T21:50:51Z"}

{"id":"tdsl-6jw1","title":"Audit ttrpg_cli host integration for reusable runtime patterns","description":"The CLI currently acts as a full host implementation on top of ttrpg_interp. It contains several integration patterns and conveniences that may be broadly useful to future hosts, but today they live in ttrpg_cli-specific code. We should examine the CLI host layer and decide which concepts belong in ttrpg_interp as reusable integration primitives versus which should remain CLI-only behavior.

Motivation:
- Avoid letting the CLI become the de facto host API.
- Keep the runtime contract defined by ttrpg_interp rather than by host-specific workarounds or conveniences.
- Make future non-CLI and non-Rust hosts easier by promoting general integration concepts into the core crate when appropriate.

Audit targets:
- RefCellState aliasing/borrow-management pattern around GameState and StateProvider.
- Mutation arithmetic and clamping logic currently handled in the CLI host.
- Prompt fallback behavior (queued values, suggest/default handling, interactive stdin behavior).
- Dice queue / seeded RNG host behaviors.
- Effect logging, entity naming, and formatting conventions that may or may not belong in core.
- Any other Layer 1 hosting patterns duplicated from or adjacent to StateAdapter behavior.

Questions to answer:
- Which CLI patterns are generally reusable host abstractions?
- Which should move into ttrpg_interp as public helpers or integration utilities?
- Which are intentionally CLI-specific and should stay out of the runtime contract?
- Are there cases where CLI behavior has implicitly shaped runtime semantics or documentation in undesirable ways?

Deliverable:
- A short design note or issue breakdown identifying candidate extractions into ttrpg_interp, recommended non-extractions, and any follow-up refactor beads needed.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"tfgast@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-03-09T21:20:50Z","created_by":"Theodore Gast","updated_at":"2026-03-09T21:20:50Z"}

new_value:
{"design":"# Audit: CLI Host Integration Patterns

## Architecture Summary

ttrpg_interp provides three core traits (StateProvider, WritableState, EffectHandler) plus a reference GameState implementation. The CLI builds a substantial host layer on top. Several patterns are generic enough to extract, while others are intentionally CLI-specific.

## Candidate Extractions

### Tier 1: Move into ttrpg_interp (or new ttrpg_host crate)

**1. RefCellState wrapper** (effects.rs:44-90)
- Generic StateProvider impl over &RefCell<GameState>
- Every host using RefCell will re-implement this identically
- Zero CLI dependencies; pure delegation
- Recommendation: Move to ttrpg_interp next to GameState

**2. TrackedInterpreter RAII** (runner/mod.rs:613-648)
- Seeds Interpreter invocation counter from GameState, persists on drop
- Any host persisting state across evaluations needs this
- Recommendation: Move to ttrpg_interp as InterpreterSession or similar

**3. compute_field_value / clamping detection** (effects.rs:249-308)
- Reads old value, applies op (+= -= =), clamps, detects clamping
- Core mutation arithmetic that any host applying MutateField needs
- Currently duplicates logic that could live near GameState.write_field
- Recommendation: Move to ttrpg_interp as a utility on MutateField or GameState

**4. Source resolution** (source_resolve.rs, 294 lines)
- BFS import traversal from entrypoint files
- No CLI dependencies (uses ttrpg_parser::extract_imports)
- Already reusable; just happens to live in CLI
- Recommendation: Move to ttrpg_parser (closest to import parsing)

**5. Manifest/package resolution** (manifest.rs, runner/load.rs)
- TOML manifest parsing and target resolution
- No CLI dependencies beyond file I/O
- Recommendation: New shared location (ttrpg_parser or standalone)

### Tier 2: Provide as optional utilities

**6. Dice queue + RNG handler** (effects.rs:164-189)
- VecDeque<i64> front-pop per die, validate range, fallback to StdRng
- Essential for deterministic testing in any host
- Recommendation: Provide as QueuedDiceProvider in ttrpg_interp; hosts can compose into their EffectHandler

**7. Prompt queue + fallback** (effects.rs:191-247)
- Queue → interactive callback → suggest → default hierarchy
- Reusable pattern but interactive callback is host-specific
- Recommendation: Provide PromptResolver trait + QueuedPromptProvider utility

**8. Entity handle registry** (runner/mod.rs handle maps)
- Bidirectional String↔EntityRef mapping
- Every user-facing host needs human-readable entity names
- Recommendation: Provide HandleRegistry utility in ttrpg_interp

### Tier 3: Consider for new ttrpg_host crate

If Tier 1+2 extractions bloat ttrpg_interp's API surface too much, a separate ttrpg_host crate could house:
- RefCellState, TrackedInterpreter, HandleRegistry
- QueuedDiceProvider, QueuedPromptProvider
- compute_field_value / mutation helpers
- Source resolution and manifest loading

This keeps ttrpg_interp focused on the interpreter core while providing a "batteries included" host integration layer.

## Recommended Non-Extractions (Stay in CLI)

**1. REPL subsystem** — reedline, completions, highlighting, validation
- Deeply CLI-specific; no other host wants terminal line editing
- Correct location

**2. CLI command grammar** (commands.rs, 978 lines)
- 50+ text commands (spawn, set, destroy, eval, assert, etc.)
- This IS the CLI's value proposition; not a general host concern

**3. Interactive stdin prompts** — raw mode toggling, terminal I/O
- Only relevant for terminal hosts

**4. Output formatting** (format.rs) — colored output, unit suffixes
- Presentation layer; hosts will have their own rendering

**5. Assert commands** (runner/assert.rs) — testing DSL
- CLI-specific testing convenience

**6. Heredoc/continuation state machine** — multi-line input accumulation
- Terminal interaction pattern only

**7. Quiet mode / interactive mode flags** — CLI UX concerns

**8. Coverage tracking** — CLI-specific reporting integration

## Cases Where CLI Shaped Runtime Semantics

1. **Prompt fallback order**: The CLI's queue→suggest→default hierarchy has become the de facto prompt contract. Other hosts may want different strategies. Extracting PromptResolver as a trait formalizes this.

2. **Clamping as logging concern**: Clamping detection lives in CLI but the bounds info comes from the interpreter. The interpreter should probably provide a utility to compute clamped results rather than making each host re-derive the arithmetic.

3. **Entity naming**: The opaque EntityRef design is correct, but the lack of any standard handle registry means every host re-invents bidirectional maps. A standard utility would codify the pattern without coupling it to the runtime.

## Recommended Follow-up Beads

1. Extract RefCellState + TrackedInterpreter into ttrpg_interp
2. Extract compute_field_value / mutation arithmetic into ttrpg_interp
3. Move source_resolve into ttrpg_parser
4. Move manifest handling to shared location
5. Add QueuedDiceProvider + QueuedPromptProvider utilities
6. Add HandleRegistry utility
7. Evaluate whether a ttrpg_host crate is warranted after items 1-6"}

{"notes":"## Potential Implementation Strategy: Suspend + Summon

Brainstormed approach: model complex transformations by composing two other planned features — suspended/off-board entities (tdsl-4qxs) and summon lifecycle (tdsl-ftim).

### Pattern
1. Suspend the original entity (off-board, preserved)
2. Spawn a form entity with the original's HP but the new form's stats
3. Link form back to original via a Polymorphed condition
4. On dispel/duration end: despawn form, restore original, sync HP

### Spell-to-model mapping
- **Polymorph Other**: Suspend + Summon (daily identity-loss check as condition hook)
- **Shape Change**: Suspend + Summon with free re-summoning of different forms
- **Polymorph Object**: Suspend + Summon with duration factor table
- **Polymorph Self**: Condition overlay only (just movement modes change, keep own stats/AC/attacks)
- **Flesh to Stone**: Suspend only (no form spawned); Stone to Flesh = restore + system shock
- **Statue**: Condition toggle (shift in/out freely, retain senses + HP)
- **Reincarnation**: Direct entity mutation (not a transformation — reroll physical stats for new race, keep mental stats + XP)

### Why this works
- Reuses tdsl-4qxs (suspend) and tdsl-ftim (summon lifecycle) — no new language concepts needed
- Identity is clean: original entity preserved perfectly, no field remapping
- Form entity is a real entity so `is` checks, condition stacking, and combat code all work naturally
- The link between original and form can be a Polymorphed condition with `original: entity` field
- Restore = on_remove hook of Polymorphed condition, syncs HP back

### Dependencies
Try this approach once tdsl-4qxs and tdsl-ftim (and their deps tdsl-i5bf, tdsl-420m) are closed."}

old_value:
{"id":"tdsl-3avp","title":"Refactor Position to use opaque host handles instead of Arc<dyn Any>","description":"Value::Position currently wraps PositionValue(pub Arc<dyn Any + Send + Sync>). This works for in-process Rust hosts, but it leaks a Rust-specific representation into the runtime boundary and makes future foreign-host, FFI, serialization, and resumable execution work harder.

We should refactor Position to be an explicitly opaque host handle rather than an arbitrary Rust object pointer.

Goals:
- Replace PositionValue(pub Arc<dyn Any + Send + Sync>) with an opaque handle type suitable for serialization/FFI (likely u64 initially).
- Update Value::Position to carry that handle type rather than Any.
- Narrow the host contract so position-specific operations work on position handles directly instead of generic &Value where practical.
- Preserve the semantic rule that the interpreter cannot inspect Position values and must delegate comparison/distance to the host.

Why:
- Removes pointer-identity behavior from the public runtime model.
- Makes the boundary more honest: positions are host-owned opaque references.
- Improves future support for foreign-language hosts and a resumable step API.
- Reduces coupling between interpreter internals and Rust-only host representations.

Likely scope:
- ttrpg_interp::value Position representation
- StateProvider::position_eq / distance signatures and call sites
- reference_state::GameState grid-position implementation
- Tests that currently construct Position values from Arc<dyn Any>

Open design question:
- Use u64 for simplicity, or choose a more process-stable/string-based opaque handle if out-of-process hosts are expected soon. Default recommendation is u64 unless stronger persistence guarantees are needed.

Definition of done:
- No public runtime Position representation depends on Any or pointer identity.
- Host position operations are expressed in terms of opaque handles.
- Existing equality/distance semantics remain host-delegated and tests are updated accordingly.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"tfgast@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-03-09T21:03:39Z","created_by":"Theodore Gast","updated_at":"2026-03-09T21:03:39Z"}

{"description":"Split out from tdsl-l23b. Add a WorldObject entity type and composable groups so spells like Hold Portal, Wizard Lock, Knock, Light, Continual Light, Darkness, Create/Destroy Water, Create Food and Water, Purify Food and Drink, Glyph of Warding, Fire Trap, and related effects can operate on modeled targets instead of GM-only narration.

## Design: Option C — Thin Entity + Optional Groups

### WorldObject Entity
```
entity WorldObject {
  name: string
  kind: ObjectKind          // Door, Portal, Chest, Torch, FoodStore, Surface, etc.
  material: ObjectMaterial  // Crystal, Glass, Leather, Metal, Paper, PotteryOrBone, RopeOrCloth, StoneOrGem, Wood
  magical: bool
  magic_bonus: int          // for item saving throw bonus calculation
  destroyed: bool
}
```

### Groups (optional mixins, same pattern as EquipmentSlots/Spellcasting)

- **Lockable** — locked, stuck, held (Hold Portal), wizard_locked (Wizard Lock), spiked
- **Illumination** — lit, radius, magical (WorldObject is the light source; it produces light in a Zone)
- **Consumable** — quantity, purified
- **Wardable** — warded, glyph_type, trigger_on_open
- **Container** — capacity, contents list (inventory transfer mechanics deferred to separate issue)

### Wiring into existing systems
- `material` field feeds into existing `item_saving_throw()` in osric_item_saves.ttrpg
- Lockable states feed into existing strength test functions in osric_ability.ttrpg (minor test for stuck doors, extraordinary for held/wizard-locked)
- Wardable builds on existing Glyph of Warding zone-trigger pattern in osric_zone_spells.ttrpg
- Illumination: WorldObject + Illumination group creates/destroys light Zones
- Dropped items: WorldObject represents items placed in the world (material field enables item saving throws from area damage)

### Key insight from OSRIC rules
OSRIC does NOT give mundane objects HP. Instead it uses:
1. Item saving throws — material-based d20 table (already implemented)
2. Strength tests for doors — d6 chance tables (already implemented)
3. Structural HP — siege scale only, not relevant here"}

{"design":"## Design: Spell Registry Scaffolding

### Problem

The current spell registry has three hand-maintained N-arm match blocks (spell_def, resolve_spell, resolve_spell_monsters) that grow linearly with every spell. Resolver signatures vary across six shapes (Character, entity, list<Character>, list<entity>, list<Monster>, and single vs multi-target), preventing clean dispatch. Zone spells have no casting flow at all — the host manually spawns Zone entities.

### Solution: Three-stage approach

#### Stage 1: Foundation (no language changes)

**1a. Normalize resolver signatures**
Fix implementation defects where spells incorrectly constrain target types (e.g. Cure Light Wounds takes Character but should work on any living creature). Unify all direct-targeted resolvers to fn(Character, list<entity>). Single-target spells unwrap internally. This eliminates the resolve_spell / resolve_spell_monsters split.

**1b. Convert spell_def to table**
Replace the derive+match with a table block. Tables already error on missing entries at runtime (the right behavior vs. silent _ => {} fallthrough).

**1c. Add zone spell resolvers**
Write DSL-side resolve functions for each zone spell that spawn Zone entities. The host provides spatial input (position, shape) via a ZonePlacement struct; the DSL owns everything else (slot consumption, source setup, entity creation).

#### Stage 2: Named function references (language feature)

Add support for storing references to top-level named functions. NOT closures — no captures, no anonymous functions, no partial application.

- Type syntax: fn(Character, list<entity>) or fn(Character, ZonePlacement) etc.
- Reference syntax: bare function name in expression position
- AST: TypeExpr::FnRef { params, return_type }, ExprKind::FnRef(name)
- Checker: resolve name, verify signature match at compile time
- Interpreter: Value::FnRef(String) — dispatches through existing call machinery
- Call syntax: normal call resolver(caster, targets)

#### Stage 3: Unified registry (combines stages 1+2)

Three resolver shapes exist, modeled as an enum with function reference payloads:

- SpellResolve.Targeted(resolve: fn(Character, list<entity>)) — Fireball, Bless, Hold Person
- SpellResolve.Zone(resolve: fn(Character, ZonePlacement)) — Silence, Wall of Fire
- SpellResolve.SelfOnly(resolve: fn(Character)) — Shield, Feather Fall

SpellEntry struct combines SpellDef + SpellResolve. A table spell_registry(SpellId) -> SpellEntry replaces all three dispatch functions. Dispatch collapses to one cast_spell function that matches on SpellResolve and delegates.

### Follow-on: Table enum exhaustiveness (checker enhancement)

Tables keyed on an enum type should optionally warn/error when not all variants are covered (like Rust match). Small checker enhancement, not blocking.

### Explicitly out of scope
- Creature taxonomy for targeting validation (tdsl-e837)
- Persistent status conditions (tdsl-42vn)
- Full closure/capture semantics
- Convention-based auto-dispatch"}

The current OSRIC spell model only wires a small subset of spells through SpellId, SpellDef, and dispatch. Add the shared metadata/registry scaffolding needed to support broad spell coverage cleanly across traditions without conflating it with individual spell effect implementation tasks. This is the schema/foundation side of expansion, distinct from implementing each spell body under tdsl-5mpl.*.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"tfgast@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-03-09T20:41:03Z","created_by":"Theodore Gast","updated_at":"2026-03-09T20:41:03Z"}

{"design":"## Design: Spell Registry Scaffolding

### Problem

The current spell registry has three hand-maintained N-arm match blocks (spell_def, resolve_spell, resolve_spell_monsters) that grow linearly with every spell. Resolver signatures vary across six shapes (Character, entity, list<Character>, list<entity>, list<Monster>, and single vs multi-target), preventing clean dispatch. Zone spells have no casting flow at all — the host manually spawns Zone entities.

### Solution: Three-stage approach

#### Stage 1: Foundation (no language changes)

**1a. Normalize resolver signatures**
Fix implementation defects where spell resolvers incorrectly constrain target types. Cure Light Wounds takes target: Character but should accept entity (works on any living creature). Hold Person takes list<Character> but works on humanoid monsters too. Cause Light Wounds already uses entity correctly. Unify all direct-targeted resolvers to fn(Character, list<entity>). Single-target spells unwrap internally. This eliminates the resolve_spell / resolve_spell_monsters split in osric_spell_registry.ttrpg. Also remove silent _ => {} fallthrough — missing routes should error.

**1b. Convert spell_def to table**
Replace the spell_def derive+match with a table block. Tables already error on missing entries at runtime, which is the correct behavior vs. the current silent fallthrough. One line per spell entry. No language changes required.

**1c. Add zone spell resolvers**
Write DSL-side resolve functions for each zone spell that spawn Zone entities. The host provides spatial input via a ZonePlacement struct; the DSL owns slot consumption, source setup, and entity creation. Add SpellId variants for all zone spells.

**1d. Structured SpellArea metadata**
Replace the free-text area_of_effect string on SpellDef with structured data so the host knows what spatial input to collect without spell-specific logic.

AreaShape enum: None, Radius(Feet), Sphere(Feet), Wall(length_per_level: Feet, thickness: Feet), Cone(length: Feet), Line(length: Feet, width: Feet), Special(string).

AnchorMode enum: PositionOnly (Glyph of Warding, Blade Barrier), EntityOnly (Protection from Evil 10 Radius — always caster), Either (Silence 15 Radius — point or creature).

SpellArea struct: shape (AreaShape) + anchor_mode (AnchorMode). Replaces the area_of_effect string field on SpellDef.

This also covers non-zone spells — Fireball has a blast radius the host needs for target selection even though it resolves as direct damage.

ZonePlacement struct for resolve functions:

ZoneAnchor enum: Position(Position) or Entity(entity). ZonePlacement struct: anchor (ZoneAnchor) + orientation (option<Direction>) for walls and cones.

The resolve function handles both anchor types — entity-anchored zones set moves_with_anchor=true and track the anchor; position-anchored zones are stationary.

#### Stage 2: Named function references (language feature)

Add support for storing references to top-level named functions as values. Scoped narrowly: no closures, no captures, no anonymous functions, no partial application. Just named references to existing functions with exact signature matching. Type syntax: fn(T1, T2) -> R. Reference: bare function name in expression position. AST additions: TypeExpr::FnRef { params, return_type }, ExprKind::FnRef(name). Checker: resolve name, verify signature match at compile time. Interpreter: Value::FnRef(String) dispatches through existing call machinery. Motivated by spell registry dispatch but general-purpose — useful for monster abilities, item effects, trap triggers, etc.

#### Stage 3: Unified registry (combines stages 1+2)

Three resolver shapes exist, modeled as an enum with function reference payloads:

- SpellResolve.Targeted(resolve: fn(Character, list<entity>)) — Fireball, Bless, Hold Person
- SpellResolve.Zone(resolve: fn(Character, ZonePlacement)) — Silence, Wall of Fire
- SpellResolve.SelfOnly(resolve: fn(Character)) — Shield, Feather Fall

SpellEntry struct combines SpellDef + SpellResolve. A table spell_registry(SpellId) -> SpellEntry replaces all three dispatch functions. Dispatch collapses to one cast_spell function that matches on SpellResolve and delegates.

### Follow-on: Table enum exhaustiveness (checker enhancement)

Tables keyed on an enum type should optionally warn/error when not all variants are covered (like Rust match). Small checker enhancement, not blocking.

### Explicitly out of scope
- Creature taxonomy for targeting validation (tdsl-e837)
- Persistent status conditions (tdsl-42vn)
- Full closure/capture semantics
- Convention-based auto-dispatch"}

{"design":"## Design: Spell Registry Scaffolding

### Problem

The current spell registry has three hand-maintained N-arm match blocks (spell_def, resolve_spell, resolve_spell_monsters) that grow linearly with every spell. Resolver signatures vary across six shapes (Character, entity, list<Character>, list<entity>, list<Monster>, and single vs multi-target), preventing clean dispatch. Zone spells have no casting flow at all — the host manually spawns Zone entities. AoE damage spells like Fireball expect pre-built target lists rather than declaring their area and prompting the host for occupants.

### Solution: Three-stage approach

#### Stage 1: Foundation (no language changes)

**1a. Normalize resolver signatures**
Fix implementation defects where spell resolvers incorrectly constrain target types. Cure Light Wounds takes target: Character but should accept entity (works on any living creature). Hold Person takes list<Character> but works on humanoid monsters too. Unify all direct-targeted resolvers to fn(Character, list<entity>). Single-target spells unwrap internally. This eliminates the resolve_spell / resolve_spell_monsters split. Remove silent _ => {} fallthrough — missing routes should error.

**1b. Convert spell_def to table**
Replace the spell_def derive+match with a table block. Tables already error on missing entries at runtime, which is the correct behavior vs. the current silent fallthrough.

**1c. Add zone spell resolvers**
Write DSL-side resolve functions for each zone spell that spawn Zone entities. Add SpellId variants for all zone spells.

**1d. Structured SpellArea metadata**
Replace the free-text area_of_effect string on SpellDef with structured data so the host knows what spatial input to collect without spell-specific logic.

AreaShape enum: None, Radius(Feet), Sphere(Feet), Wall(length_per_level: Feet, thickness: Feet), Cone(length: Feet), Line(length: Feet, width: Feet), Special(string).

AnchorMode enum: PositionOnly (Glyph of Warding, Blade Barrier), EntityOnly (Protection from Evil 10 Radius — always caster), Either (Silence 15 Radius — point or creature).

SpellArea struct (shape + anchor_mode) replaces the area_of_effect string field on SpellDef.

ZoneAnchor enum: Position(Position) or Entity(entity). ZonePlacement struct: anchor (ZoneAnchor) + orientation (option<Direction>) for walls/cones.

**1e. AoE spells use prompt for occupant resolution**
AoE spells like Fireball should declare their area via SpellArea metadata and use the existing prompt mechanism to ask the host who is in the area, instead of taking a pre-built list<entity>. This works for theater-of-the-mind (GM picks), physical table (GM picks), and VTT (auto-resolves). Once spatial query infrastructure (tdsl-586) matures, prompts could be auto-resolved.

This unifies AoE damage spells and zone spells under the same Area input shape — both take ZonePlacement, the difference is just instant vs persistent effect. Spells with explicit target selection (Magic Missile, Cure Light Wounds) remain Targeted with list<entity>.

#### Stage 2: Named function references (language feature)

Add support for storing references to top-level named functions as values. Scoped narrowly: no closures, no captures, no anonymous functions, no partial application. Just named references to existing functions with exact signature matching. Type syntax: fn(T1, T2) -> R. Reference: bare function name in expression position. AST: TypeExpr::FnRef, ExprKind::FnRef(name). Checker: resolve name, verify signature match. Interpreter: Value::FnRef(String) dispatches through existing call machinery.

#### Stage 3: Unified registry (combines stages 1+2)

Three resolver shapes, modeled as an enum with function reference payloads:

- SpellResolve.Targeted(resolve: fn(Character, list<entity>)) — caster chooses individual targets explicitly. Magic Missile (distribute missiles), Cure Light Wounds (touch one creature), Hold Person (pick 1-3 humanoids).
- SpellResolve.Area(resolve: fn(Character, ZonePlacement)) — spatial targeting. Fireball prompts for occupants and deals instant damage; Silence spawns a persistent Zone. Same input, different persistence.
- SpellResolve.SelfOnly(resolve: fn(Character)) — caster-only spells like Shield, Feather Fall.

SpellEntry struct combines SpellDef + SpellResolve. A table spell_registry(SpellId) -> SpellEntry replaces all three dispatch functions. Dispatch collapses to one cast_spell function.

### Follow-on: Table enum exhaustiveness (checker enhancement)

Tables keyed on an enum type should optionally warn/error when not all variants are covered. Small checker enhancement, not blocking.

{"id":"tdsl-fzir","title":"Add spell metadata and registry scaffolding for broad spell coverage","description":"Split out from tdsl-l23b. The current OSRIC spell model only wires a small subset of spells through SpellId, SpellDef, and dispatch. Add the shared metadata/registry scaffolding needed to support broad spell coverage cleanly across traditions without conflating it with individual spell effect implementation tasks. This is the schema/foundation side of expansion, distinct from implementing each spell body under tdsl-5mpl.*.","design":"## Design: Spell Registry Scaffolding

### Problem

The current spell registry has three hand-maintained N-arm match blocks (spell_def, resolve_spell, resolve_spell_monsters) that grow linearly with every spell. Resolver signatures vary across six shapes (Character, entity, list<Character>, list<entity>, list<Monster>, and single vs multi-target), preventing clean dispatch. Zone spells have no casting flow at all — the host manually spawns Zone entities.

### Solution: Three-stage approach

#### Stage 1: Foundation (no language changes)

**1a. Normalize resolver signatures**
Fix implementation defects where spell resolvers incorrectly constrain target types. Cure Light Wounds takes target: Character but should accept entity (works on any living creature). Hold Person takes list<Character> but works on humanoid monsters too. Cause Light Wounds already uses entity correctly. Unify all direct-targeted resolvers to fn(Character, list<entity>). Single-target spells unwrap internally. This eliminates the resolve_spell / resolve_spell_monsters split in osric_spell_registry.ttrpg. Also remove silent _ => {} fallthrough — missing routes should error.

**1b. Convert spell_def to table**
Replace the spell_def derive+match with a table block. Tables already error on missing entries at runtime, which is the correct behavior vs. the current silent fallthrough. One line per spell entry. No language changes required.

**1c. Add zone spell resolvers**
Write DSL-side resolve functions for each zone spell that spawn Zone entities. The host provides spatial input via a ZonePlacement struct; the DSL owns slot consumption, source setup, and entity creation. Add SpellId variants for all zone spells.

**1d. Structured SpellArea metadata**
Replace the free-text area_of_effect string on SpellDef with structured data so the host knows what spatial input to collect without spell-specific logic.

AreaShape enum: None, Radius(Feet), Sphere(Feet), Wall(length_per_level: Feet, thickness: Feet), Cone(length: Feet), Line(length: Feet, width: Feet), Special(string).

AnchorMode enum: PositionOnly (Glyph of Warding, Blade Barrier), EntityOnly (Protection from Evil 10 Radius — always caster), Either (Silence 15 Radius — point or creature).

SpellArea struct: shape (AreaShape) + anchor_mode (AnchorMode). Replaces the area_of_effect string field on SpellDef.

This also covers non-zone spells — Fireball has a blast radius the host needs for target selection even though it resolves as direct damage.

ZonePlacement struct for resolve functions:

ZoneAnchor enum: Position(Position) or Entity(entity). ZonePlacement struct: anchor (ZoneAnchor) + orientation (option<Direction>) for walls and cones.

The resolve function handles both anchor types — entity-anchored zones set moves_with_anchor=true and track the anchor; position-anchored zones are stationary.

#### Stage 2: Named function references (language feature)

Add support for storing references to top-level named functions as values. Scoped narrowly: no closures, no captures, no anonymous functions, no partial application. Just named references to existing functions with exact signature matching. Type syntax: fn(T1, T2) -> R. Reference: bare function name in expression position. AST additions: TypeExpr::FnRef { params, return_type }, ExprKind::FnRef(name). Checker: resolve name, verify signature match at compile time. Interpreter: Value::FnRef(String) dispatches through existing call machinery. Motivated by spell registry dispatch but general-purpose — useful for monster abilities, item effects, trap triggers, etc.

#### Stage 3: Unified registry (combines stages 1+2)

Three resolver shapes exist, modeled as an enum with function reference payloads:

- SpellResolve.Targeted(resolve: fn(Character, list<entity>)) — Fireball, Bless, Hold Person
- SpellResolve.Zone(resolve: fn(Character, ZonePlacement)) — Silence, Wall of Fire
- SpellResolve.SelfOnly(resolve: fn(Character)) — Shield, Feather Fall

SpellEntry struct combines SpellDef + SpellResolve. A table spell_registry(SpellId) -> SpellEntry replaces all three dispatch functions. Dispatch collapses to one cast_spell function that matches on SpellResolve and delegates.

### Follow-on: Table enum exhaustiveness (checker enhancement)

Tables keyed on an enum type should optionally warn/error when not all variants are covered (like Rust match). Small checker enhancement, not blocking.

### Explicitly out of scope
- Creature taxonomy for targeting validation (tdsl-e837)
- Persistent status conditions (tdsl-42vn)
- Full closure/capture semantics
- Convention-based auto-dispatch","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"tfgast@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-03-09T20:41:03Z","created_by":"Theodore Gast","updated_at":"2026-03-10T20:29:07Z"}

{"description":"Fix implementation defects where spell resolvers incorrectly constrain target types AND caster types. Two changes:

1. TARGET SIGNATURES: Cure Light Wounds takes target: Character but should accept entity (works on any living creature). Hold Person takes list<Character> but works on humanoid monsters too. Cause Light Wounds already uses entity correctly. Unify all direct-targeted resolvers to fn(entity, list<entity>). Single-target spells unwrap internally. This eliminates the resolve_spell / resolve_spell_monsters split in osric_spell_registry.ttrpg.

2. CASTER SIGNATURES: All resolvers take caster: Character, but monsters with spell-like abilities also need to cast. Change caster param to entity across all resolvers. Generalize caster_level() to be entity-aware — check for Character class levels or Monster SpellAbility group. This must happen before adding broad spell coverage, otherwise every resolver gets rewritten later.

Also remove silent _ => {} fallthrough — missing routes should error.

Final canonical signatures for SpellResolve: fn(entity, list<entity>) for Targeted, fn(entity, ZonePlacement) for Area, fn(entity) for SelfOnly."}

{"design":"## Design: Spell Registry Scaffolding

### Problem

The current spell registry has three hand-maintained N-arm match blocks (spell_def, resolve_spell, resolve_spell_monsters) that grow linearly with every spell. Resolver signatures vary across six shapes (Character, entity, list<Character>, list<entity>, list<Monster>, and single vs multi-target), preventing clean dispatch. Zone spells have no casting flow at all — the host manually spawns Zone entities. AoE damage spells like Fireball expect pre-built target lists rather than declaring their area and prompting the host for occupants.

### Solution: Three-stage approach

#### Stage 1: Foundation (no language changes)

**1a. Normalize resolver signatures**
Fix implementation defects where spell resolvers incorrectly constrain target AND caster types. Change caster param from Character to entity (supports monster spell-like abilities). Unify all direct-targeted resolvers to fn(entity, list<entity>). This eliminates the resolve_spell / resolve_spell_monsters split. Remove silent _ => {} fallthrough — missing routes should error.

**1b. Convert spell_def to table**
Replace the spell_def derive+match with a table block. Tables already error on missing entries at runtime, which is the correct behavior vs. the current silent fallthrough.

**1c. Add zone spell resolvers**
Write DSL-side resolve functions for each zone spell that spawn Zone entities. Add SpellId variants for all zone spells.

**1d. Structured SpellArea metadata**
Replace the free-text area_of_effect string on SpellDef with structured data so the host knows what spatial input to collect without spell-specific logic.

AreaShape enum: None, Radius(Feet), Sphere(Feet), Wall(length_per_level: Feet, thickness: Feet), Cone(length: Feet), Line(length: Feet, width: Feet), Special(string).

AnchorMode enum: PositionOnly (Glyph of Warding, Blade Barrier), EntityOnly (Protection from Evil 10 Radius — always caster), Either (Silence 15 Radius — point or creature).

SpellArea struct (shape + anchor_mode) replaces the area_of_effect string field on SpellDef.

Generic spatial targeting primitives (used by spells, breath weapons, and any future AoE):

AnchorPoint enum: Position(Position) or Entity(entity).
Placement struct: anchor (AnchorPoint) + orientation (option<Direction>) for walls/cones.

These are not zone-specific — they describe any spatial targeting input from the host.

**1e. AoE spells use prompt for occupant resolution**
AoE spells like Fireball should declare their area via SpellArea metadata and use the existing prompt mechanism to ask the host who is in the area, instead of taking a pre-built list<entity>. This works for theater-of-the-mind, physical table, and VTT equally.

This unifies AoE damage spells and zone spells under the same Area input shape — both take Placement, the difference is just instant vs persistent effect. Spells with explicit target selection (Magic Missile, Cure Light Wounds) remain Targeted with list<entity>.

#### Stage 2: Named function references (language feature)

Add support for storing references to top-level named functions as values. Scoped narrowly: no closures, no captures, no anonymous functions, no partial application. Just named references to existing functions with exact signature matching. Type syntax: fn(T1, T2) -> R. Reference: bare function name in expression position. AST: TypeExpr::FnRef, ExprKind::FnRef(name). Checker: resolve name, verify signature match. Interpreter: Value::FnRef(String) dispatches through existing call machinery.

#### Stage 3: Unified registry (combines stages 1+2)

Three resolver shapes, modeled as an enum with function reference payloads:

- SpellResolve.Targeted(resolve: fn(entity, list<entity>)) — caster chooses individual targets explicitly. Magic Missile (distribute missiles), Cure Light Wounds (touch one creature), Hold Person (pick 1-3 humanoids).
- SpellResolve.Area(resolve: fn(entity, Placement)) — spatial targeting. Fireball prompts for occupants and deals instant damage; Silence spawns a persistent Zone. Same input, different persistence.
- SpellResolve.SelfOnly(resolve: fn(entity)) — caster-only spells like Shield, Feather Fall.

SpellEntry struct combines SpellDef + SpellResolve. A table spell_registry(SpellId) -> SpellEntry replaces all three dispatch functions. Dispatch collapses to one cast_spell function.

### Follow-on: Table enum exhaustiveness (checker enhancement)

Tables keyed on an enum type should optionally warn/error when not all variants are covered. Small checker enhancement, not blocking.

### Explicitly out of scope
- Creature taxonomy for targeting validation (tdsl-e837)
- Persistent status conditions (tdsl-42vn)
- Full closure/capture semantics
- Convention-based auto-dispatch"}
